Already covered via examples and documentation.

By default, the IRC library does attempt to decode all incoming streams as UTF-8, but I acknowledge that there are cases where decoding is undesirable or a custom decoding option is desirable. To support these cases, since irc 3.4.2, the ServerConnection class may be customized. The 'buffer_class' attribute on the ServerConnection determines what class is used for buffering lines from the input stream. By default it is DecodingLineBuffer, but may be re-assigned with another class, such as irc.client.LineBuffer, which does not decode the lines and passes them through as byte strings. The 'buffer_class' attribute may be assigned for all instances of ServerConnection by overriding the class attribute::

    irc.client.ServerConnection.buffer_class = irc.client.LineBuffer

or it may be overridden on a per-instance basis (as long as it's overridden before the connection is established)::

    server = irc.client.IRC().server()
    server.buffer_class = irc.client.LineBuffer
    server.connect()

I've added a section to the README that documents these options.

Does this interface provide the option you seek? If not, please re-open.

I'm not willing to have that much complexity built in to the `run()` method.   Run currently operates as follows:

1. run(coro).  Runs the specified coroutine to completion and returns its result.
2. run(). Runs the kernel for a single scheduling cycle, executing work from background tasks (if any), and returns.

That's basically it.  If an optional timeout is given, it imposes a deadline either by causing a TaskTimeout exception to be delivered to the supplied coroutine or by supplying the timeout argument to the `select()` call used for I/O polling.

I don't really want to have it do anything more than this.
I'm not sure it needs to run inside thread_pool.  I was thinking more about it being initialized/shutdown in a manner similar to how thread pools do it.     It could still be a separate thing.

Let have it outlined:
- run() exits when main task returns, keeping other task.
- An explicit run(shutdown=true) or Kernel context exit will cancel all tasks.

I'm going to merge this, but some changes will be made to the *args, **kwargs calling convention.   Throughout Curio, there has been effort to standardize the calling convention of functions that run other functions (e.g., `spawn(func, *args)`, `run_in_thread(func, *args)`, etc.).  Positional arguments are passed to the function itself whereas keyword arguments are used for configuration and options to the operation itself.   To pass keyword arguments, you would use `partial()`.

Ok, so I've been poking around a bit using the `pdb` module.

When I use np.floatXX (where XX != 64), Numpy tries to access `Quantity.__array_struct__`. This calls `Quantity.__getattr__`, which calls `compat._to_magnitude`, which ultimately calls `np.asarray` on the quantity's magnitude. So this magnitude, cast to an ndarray (but otherwise not checked for nondimensionality) is what gets returned to whatever numpy code is asking for `__array_struct__`.

Note that this also makes `np.float32(u.V)` valid, while `float(u.V)` raises `DimensionalityError`.

Interestingly, `np.float64` calls `Quantity.__float__` first, so if the quantity is dimensionless, it is converted appropriately and returned. However, if `__float__` raises an exception, `np.float64` catches it, ignores it, and tries `Quantity.__array_struct__` instead. Thus, calling `np.float64(u.V)` does not raise an exception, but instead returns `1.0`.

I don't know why this is happening on my system and not yours. The solution on my end would be to check for (non)dimensionality when looking for `__array_x` attributes, but I'm not sure if that would cause existing code to fail.
It is not possible because of the way it is implemented (units is a dictionary, and the units are the keys). You can create a new unit if you like ('whatever_moles').

`Quantity(string)` calls the quantity constructor and therefore the output is a `Quantity`. `Registry.parse_expression(string)` is more generic, as it parses and evaluates a mathematical expression involving units. Under the hood, `Quantity(string)` calls `Registry.parse_expression(string)` but returns the output wrapped into a `Quantity` (because that is what the user is asking for).

I did some more review of the EnOcean EEP document and concluded that my initial assumption seems to be correct: The HVAC receiver identifies the message based on the **sender's** address, which is learnt and stored during teach-in.

Please refer to Section 1.7 of the EEP document (Teach-in procedures):
> For this purpose of a determined relationship between transmitter and receiver each transmitting device has a unique Sender-ID which is part of each radio telegram.

Also, looking in Section 1.7.1 of EnOceanSerialProtocol3_V1.17.pdf, Table 4 states that the destination address is always 0xffffffff for packets except ADT, i.e. 4BS (RORG 0xA5), which is obviously used in my devices.

Therefore, if one "server device" wants to control multiple "clients" it needs to use different Sender-IDs to ensure that the clients do not react on a packet, which is intended for a different client.

I also raised this question to Micropelt and will post their response here once (and if) I get one.

No sure, I think I understood the idea of this ticket and it also sounds logical to me. However:

What I recognized by sniffing my EnOcean packets from the single STC-MSG device to 2 different iTRVs devices is: 
The two iTRVs send individual requests to the STC-MSG using their different source addresses (direction 1 of EEP A5-20-01) (of course, not synchronous and not simultanous). When the single STC-MSG device answers each request (direction 2 of A5-20-01), this packet had different **source** addresses of the single STR-MSG device, depending to which iTRV it was sent. 

Whether these packets have also different destination addresses which were masked then to broadcasts from the USB dongle (because this was shown in the packet dump as destination) - I do not know.
I assumed they were really sent to broadcast and the receiving iTRVs recognized whether they are affected by the (learnt-in) source address of the message.

Actually the single STC-MSG heating controller device uses 9 different **source** addresses: 1 address for each HVAC actuator it controls and and 9th one for sending its status (EEP A5-11-02).

I personally don't use pandoc-crossref so I don't know. But my guess is it does. pantable with csv is design to be like another table extension of pandoc. For each cell you write in markdown normally, and it uses pandoc to parse each cell as markdown, and construct an AST table. So I think you can **invoke pantable filter first and then pandoc-crossref**. After pantable's filter, what "pandoc-crossref sees" is the JSON AST with a native pandoc table, and it wouldn't matter where it was originate from.

Let me know if it works after trying that.

I will think about that a bit more.

To explain why extension is a good idea abeit seems overkill for now: it is because I have a lot of things in my mind that I want to do with pantable. pantable and pantable2csv are decided to make csv table "almost" a first class table syntax in pandoc (pandoc offered 4 syntaxes for tables, there were discussions to add csv syntax as an official pandoc table extension, but it doesn't pass the "markdown-ish" criteria for `@jgm`. i.e. readable as plain text). This design is nice, and have been very useful to be an intermediate format when I need  to batch convert a bunch of docx to md. Furthermore, its usefulness seems to attract quite a few users. (just to add a few words, I design it so that the csv table syntax capture all possibilities given by the pandoc AST, which at the time it is written all 4 pandoc table extensions fell short of. But since I spoke with `@jgm` for the matter, he quickly add a syntax such that the grid table supports everything the AST support.)

Anyway, pantable and pantable2csv are designed with these in mind, so that more careful thoughts are given to syntaxes and its behavior to mimic a pandoc experience (e.g. you almost cannot trigger a Python exception, perhaps except for a malformed yaml syntax. warning messages are given and it will try its best to proceed).

But there are other things lesser "careful thoughts" should be given to allow for innovations, such as this. If you think about it, it is how pandoc behaves. Official features are almost always well-thought but takes tons of time to be immeplemented. By allowing extensions/filters, less rigorous process can be allowed while keeping the upstream clean.

OK so I spent a bunch of time investigating this; the pandas-ply symbolic code is pretty tricky to wrap my head around. I did, however, find a solution to this.

On the develop branch you can see in the most recent few commits some changes to the code. The nth function is in there, and uses a try/except block. I haven't seen your nth function code, but I think that the problem that was probably happening to you as well had something to do with the order of evaluation happening with the symbols. 

I've added a new decorator called `make_symbolic` (may rename it later, not sure if I like that name) which is designed to wrap a function and any symbolic arguments it has in a `symbolic.sym_call` function call. This appears to make sure that the function is chained together correctly with the other operations.  

I took a long look at the pandas-ply code, particularly the `sym_call` function, and figured out that functions taking symbols as arguments sometimes, but not always evaluate correctly. Wrapping the function with `sym_call` explicitly turns the function into a chainable `pandas_ply.symbolic.Call` object.

The nth code now is:
```python
@make_symbolic
def nth(series, n, order_by=None):
    if order_by is not None:
        series = order_series_by(series, order_by)
    try:
        return series.iloc[n]
    except:
        return np.nan
```

And does not cause an indexing error.

I'm definitely keeping this in development for now, since I'm still somewhat uncertain of the structure of the decorator and how symbolics are getting evaluated (despite the tests all passing).

Cheers,

Original comment by: [mgoblue80](http://bitbucket.org/mgoblue80)

You're right. `pytest-runner` doesn't do anything during install. In fact, it doesn't do anything during any built-in setuptools or distutils command. Instead, it provides its own setup command 'ptr'.

pytest-runner cannot be in test_requirements because it must be present before test_requirements is honored. It must necessarily already be installed in order to supply the setuptools plugin to supply the 'ptr' command (which in turn honors test_requirements and runs the tests). It's a shame that it must also be present for installing the package even though it's not invoked.

Perhaps something else can be done. Maybe it would make sense to only add the setup requirement when 'ptr' appears in sys.argv, although that feels hacky. What is the motivation for changing it?

@petri Did you end up writing a library or find one?

> GraphViz is distributed separately, and is available in package managers for the most widely used operating systems. As far as I can tell, it is used as an external program by the Python package graphviz, so no need to compile any bindings. Therefore no need to install it as part of the graphviz package.

I am well aware of the Python standards, considering that I'm a maintainer on a number of Python projects. Aside from the standards, this type of external dependency wastes developer time when a library is included as a dependency but does not provide all of the functionality required for the library to work. I remember back to the days of easy_install when you would set aside 30 minutes or more just to install extensions with a c library.

What I am suggesting is why pypandoc provides: a method that can be invoked at runtime to automatically download pypandoc if it is not installed. This is completely within the Python standards because it is not invoked on pip installation (though I do personally violate this rule on some of my packages).

```
def remove_dublicate_dicts(iterable):
    """Only keep one copy of each dict with the exact same key/value
    pairs.
    :rtype: list of dicts.
    """
    s = set()
    for d in iterable:
        hashable_dict = tuple((key, value) for key, value in d.items())
        s.add(hashable_dict)
    return [dict(item) for item in s]
```

client = Client(return_connection_error=True)

@Carreau I made a mistake with packaging and had to remove the tgz. I think if nobody asks for it I'll just leave it that way for now. Otherwise I have to do a new release (0.10.1).

> And iso-8859-1 is being used as default one, unless we use UTF-8 with BOM or forced encoding from file header.

I agree, this is wrong. I disagree however that it should much with `Script`s default. The default in Python 3 is `utf-8` and therefore it should also be UTF-8 in all files.

> So I need to somehow refactor get_statement_for_position method of the BaseNode class.

No please don't. I don't think you need to refactor it. What I suggested is that you need a similar function. But this function doesn't need to live in the syntax tree business logic.

> My first question is probably stupid, but still. Why not use some graph (or more specific tree) library, that already have many of such functions, dfs, bfs, traversals etc? Your node classes could remain mostly intact, just all methods of Node that involves children could be function on graph.

It's not a graph, it's a tree. Therefore we have something incredibly unspectacular to work with. DFS is something you can write in like 2s. Jedi has very strong performance requirements in terms of memory and performance. A library is therefore probably a bad idea. I think that trees (contrary to graphs) are so easy to work with that you really don't need a library. However it really doesn't matter, since we're stuck with what we're having anyways.

> And what I need now is to refactor get_statement_for_position method to use this functions and then to add new search function somewhere that would find 'element' for position. How this 'element' properly called by the way?

This element can be called like 50 different ways. Therefore it will be more important to work correctly with positions. It's probably better to do a different kind of search: Search the leaf under the cursor (careful it might be whitespace, in that case take the next leaf?) and then you look for parents until you are not allowed to anymore (this is limited by a certain set of node names, see https://docs.python.org/3/reference/grammar.html).

I don't think a variation of get_statement_for_position is a good solution.

> but why nodes have both type field if they have python type?

Well, because not all nodes have a Python type. Also you seem to come from a very "healthy" world. You talk about legitimate usages and so on. Don't underestimate historical issues. A lot of the things in Jedi are not ideal, because at some point we had to refactor. The refactorings were not always perfect so you have certain issues within the code. Okay, I thought about it, and sending the empty update-element immediately and sending the new hash with all later presence updates is the fastest route to a consistent state, since the last presence stanza from each resource is cached by the server and sent out in response to presence probes, this means that setting the update element empty, will make the state consistent: The resource that uploaded the new avatar will send out the presence with the new hash, this presence is cached and sent out as part of the reply to presence probes, if all other resources set the update element empty (which means it is ignored in the processing), means that (newly connecting) peers will observe a consistent state, since only in the presence of one resource hash is set and that hash corresponds to the avatar image in the vCard. Further, this consistent state is reached faster than in the case where all other resources first download the vCard and calculate the hash before they send a new presence with the new hash.

Okay yes, there are actually several problems with "git fetch --prune":

1. "git fetch --prune" will remove branches that are not merged.  If you push a branch, and a colleague deletes it on your behalf from the server to unpublish it, then when you "fetch --prune" your local branch will be deleted, even though it has not been merged.
2. "git fetch --prune" will *only* delete the branch the first time it notices the tracking branch was deleted.  So if you run "git fetch" and wind up deleting several tracking branches in the process, a subsequent run of "git fetch --prune" will remove none of the local branches.

1 would not be a problem if only it confirmed the branch was contained in another branch first.  That is somewhat arbitrary, so maybe it should be configurable which branches it should be contained in (i.e. default to master and develop).

2 is a problem because it pulls the rug out from under the feature.  I run git fetch frequently, just to sample the state on the origin.  I'm not going to make --prune the default, because of issue 1.  I would have been happy if I could fetch, review the deleted branches, *then* prune.  But that's not supported.

Basically, I think git fetch --prune is itself an incomplete feature, so I wouldn't use it for this problem as-is.

```python
class BridgePyroute2(Bridge):

    '''
    Attributes
    ----------
    id : str
        Name of the bridge.
    bridge: pyroute2.ipdb.interface.Interface
    '''
    def _start(self, bridge_dev_name=None, switch=False):
        self.bridge_dev_name = bridge_dev_name

        try:
            self.bridge = get_ipdb_singleton().create(kind='bridge', ifname=self.bridge_dev_name)
            # See Also: https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/issues/254
            # use pyroute2 functional style
            (self.bridge.set_br_ageing_time(0)
             .up()
             )
        except (NetlinkError, CreateException) as e:
            raise NetworkBackendStartError("Could not create the bridge with name '%s' in hub mode!" % self.bridge_dev_name, caused_by=e)

    def add_if(self, _if_name, if_up = True):
        try:
            tap_dev = get_ipdb_singleton().interfaces[_if_name]

            if if_up:
                tap_dev.up()
            self.bridge.up()
            self.bridge.add_port(tap_dev['index'])

        except (AttributeError, KeyError) as e:
            raise NetworkBackendBridgedBridgeError("""Could not add interface '%s' to bridge '%s'
            Bridge dump:
            %s
            Interface dump:
            %s
            Used tap devices:
            %s
            """ % (_if_name, self, check_output(["brctl", "show"]), pformat(Bridge.get_interfaces()),
                   pformat(singletons.network_backend._tap_id_mapping)), caused_by=e)
```


```python
class ConnectionPyroute2(Connection):

    def raise_lookup_device_error(self, tap_dev_name, caused_by=None):
        raise NetworkBackendConnectionError("Device '%s' not found!" % tap_dev_name, caused_by=caused_by)

    def lookup_device(self, tap_dev_name):
        try:
            idx_tap_x = get_ipdb_singleton().interfaces[tap_dev_name]
            return idx_tap_x

        except KeyError as e:
            raise self.raise_lookup_device_error(tap_dev_name, caused_by=e)

    def _tap_link_up(self, tap_x, tap_y, up=True):

        dev = self.lookup_device(tap_x)
        if up:
            dev.up()
        else:
            dev.down()
```

TL;DR: Adding GRE interfaces (or perhaps, any interface with an MTU lower than the bridge's) to a bridge lowers its MTU to the added interface's MTU, and pyroute2 throws a fit when this change when the MTU changes while committing other changes (such as adding a port to a second interface.)

Here is a reasonable workaround for this:
```python
if testbr.mtu < testvp0.mtu:
    waited = 0.0
    while testbr.mtu != testvp0.mtu and waited < 1.0:
        waited += 0.001
        time.sleep(0.001)
testbr.mtu == testvp0.mtu
```

Legendas.tv website often updates the rar files with more subtitles for more releases for a given title.

Subliminal uses the "last update" timestamp to know if the cache should be discarded or not. But we found out that for several situations, legendas.tv website updates the rar file without updating the "last update" timestamp (argh!). But they update the archive name to include the new words for the added releases.

So, if the name is part of the cache key, subliminal won't get a cache hit when that happens, and the new subtitle will be detected and evaluated.

> Do you think there's a way to support twisted interface without changing this core design? My main concern is keeping up w changes to official client so that's why I'm hesitant to change the core loop.

Almost.

The big change you have to make is not to adopt Twisted APIs like Deferred. The change you need to make is to change the I/O interface from one which calls `recv` to one which *feeds in some data*.  The best example of this in a *totally* platform-agnostic library is the https://github.com/python-hyper/hyper-h2 library from @Lukasa.

Luckily, since `recv` can always return a short read – and if your code isn't dealing with that, it's buggy – there is a simple way to do this with your existing code: replace all real socket usage with a fake object whose `recv` raises `EAGAIN` if no data is available, and returns whatever data is available.  Then, have an external API which feeds the data in and cranks the loop manually.

This does create a situation where calling those APIs directly can lead to the `EAGAIN` exception will bubble up to the caller.  But you can address this in two ways:

1. provide two implementations of I/O, one which pulls data in from the main loop, turning the crank on a python-kafka object in Twisted's `dataReceived` method, for example, or
2. use the async implementation of I/O by providing facade objects for use on non-main threads, and use something like Twisted's [`blockingCallFromThread`](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/15.5.0/api/twisted.internet.threads.html#blockingCallFromThread) to implement `Future.get` and your kafka subscriber iterator's `next` (py3: `__next__`) method.

Does this make sense?

PDF is a display-oriented language, so most of what's in the files are instructions, like 'show an 'e' in Arial-10 font at this x-y position'. PDFminer returns those details--and it's important to note that they aren't present in 'image' based pdfs that haven't been OCR'ed etc. So the 'input' are characters with bounding boxes . Hi @Francks11!

First off, thank you for your continued interest in helping improve this plugin. Although I appreciate this bug fix, I don't think this is a proper fix for this edge case. I'm calling this an edge case because I know you don't plan on making the table sortable. Because of that, having a `colspan` within the `tbody` is no big deal. The problem is that **tablesorter does not currently support sorting of `colspan` & `rowspan` within the `tbody`**.

In the [shared demo](http://jsfiddle.net/856bzzeL/329/), if you sort *any* column in the table the `colspan` row sorts to the bottom. In this specific case, it would be possible to keep the row at the top by setting the [`emptyTo`](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#emptyto) and [`stringTo`](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#stringto) options to `top` ([demo](http://jsfiddle.net/856bzzeL/330/)). This only works because the first column has numeric values and the `colspan` row has text.

Currently, I recommend using an "info-only" tbody to place fixed rows at the top of the table. And yes, there is a bug in the scroller code because it targets the first  row in the first `tbody`. It should be skipping the info-only `tbody`... so that will need to be fixed.

That sounds like a good contender for how to approach this, @bear. You could *almost* do what you described right now with pdtContext, except that the accuracy flags do not convey any details about the ambiguity of the actual text that matched. 

So unfortunately while you could exclude terms where `accuracy == pdtContext.ACU_DAY` that not only would drop *6th* (assuming pdt parsed that as a date), but also unambiguous terms like *today* and *Saturday*. That's a pretty good solution for people parsing data that contains only absolute dates but it won't work for every use case.

A measure of ambiguity could be used to ignore standalone numeric dates as well as questionable abbreviations like *sat* which may just be the past tense of *sit* and *sun* which may be a reference to our nearest star. It gets quite tricky to think of a proper way to do that.

I found [our earlier conversation](https://github.com/bear/parsedatetime/pull/92) that touched on these ideas for reference.

It seems that stdout must be closed, duped to `stderr` or piped to `/dev/null` for `xclip` work properly. So I worked around this problem with `multiprocess`:

```python
import multiprocessing
import pyperclip
import os

def pyperclip_copy_wrapper(text):
    # Dup stdout to devnull
    fdnull = os.open(os.devnull, os.O_WRONLY)
    os.dup2(fdnull, 1)
    try:
        pyperclip.copy(text)
    finally:
        os.close(1)
        os.close(fdnull)

def copy_to_clipboard(text):
    p = multiprocessing.Process(name='copy-to-clipboard', target=pyperclip_copy_wrapper, args=(text,))
    p.start()
    p.join()
    return p.exitcode
```

This works with both `xclip` and `xsel` on both Python 2.7 and Python 3.6. Hopefully it does not break anything else.

Additional note ... if you want to write down the cash and portfolio values at each point in time, you'd  do the following to access the values

````python
    import backtrader as bt
    import backtrader.observers as btobs

    # ssome things

    class MyStrategy(bt.Strategy):
        # init and other methods
        def next(self):
            print('cash at this point in time is %.2f' % self.stats.broker.cash[0])
            print('value at this point in time is %.2f' % self.stats.broker.value[0])
            print('drawdown at this point in time is %.2f' % self.stats.drawdown.drawdown[0])
            print('maxdrawdown at this point in time is %.2f' % self.stats.drawdown.maxdrawdown[0])

    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()
    cerebro.addobserver(btobs.DrawDown)

    # some other things

    cerebro.run()

    cerebro.plot()
````

Even with partial executions implemented the standard `observers` work. A small correction to the new partial execution was needed to consider the order side for the value returned by a volume filler.

The sample for it has been update with a chart which shows the observers working.

  - http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2016-07-14-volume-filling/volume-filling/

The failure may have been in your own implementation of partial executions. The chart is below

![](http://www.backtrader.com/_images/volumefilling1.png)

As long as trade isn't closed no value makes sense, because it isn't closed and therefore the most sensible value is a `null` value which is invalid. Any other value would produce the false sensation that a trade may be closed because the `closing datetime` can already be retrieved.

You should check before is `trade.isclosed`

iperf3 maintainer here.  The reason you can't get the JSON programmatically is because libiperf gets the JSON rendering from `cJSON_Print()` in the cJSON library.  cJSON owns the memory, so we want to ship this off before we have `cJSON_Delete()` clean up.  We do this all in `iperf_json_finish()`.  It doesn't necessarily have to be this way.  We could in theory (as in "I haven't tried this") make a copy of the string before calling `cJSON_Delete()`, so you could access it after the test is done.  We'd need to make sure to get rid of the string when we delete the `iperf_test` object.

I guess the other issue to be handled is you'd need to find out whether you can actually use this or not (assume we implement the correct setters and getters for `json_output_string` if we don't have them already).  I haven't thought of a good way to do that with the API as it currently stands.

If this is of interest, you can try putting an issue (or even better a pull request!) in the iperf3 issue tracker at https://github.com/esnet/iperf.

(I guess I should also mention that I am not sure if this would solve the OP's problem.)

This is a proposal for some xml to record conditional glyph substitutions in designspace.

- I'm not sure if this covers all the conditions allowed by OpenType 1.8
- this is not proposal for feature language.
- just covers single glyphs, not groups. 
- does not cover GPOS-like things. 

```
<!-- optional: list of substitution rules -->
	<rules>
		<rule name="vertical bars" enabled="true">
			<sub name="cent" byname="cent.alt"/>
			<sub name="dollar" byname="dollar.alt"/>
			<condition tag="wght" operator=">" value="250.000000"/>
			<condition tag="wdth" operator="<" value="100"/>
			<condition tag="opsz" operator=">" value="40"/>
		</rule>
    </rules>
```
## rules
`rules` element: groups all the rules. Contains one or more `rule` elements

## rule
`rule` element: a single rule. Contains one or more `sub` elements, and one or more `condition` elements. All conditions must evaluate to `true` for the rule to be
-  `name`: attribute, optional. String. Descriptive name for this rule.
- `enabled`: attribute, optional. Bool. If this rule should be evaluated or not.

## sub
`sub` element: a single glyph to glyph substitution. 
- `name` attribute, required. Name of the source glyph. 
- `byname` attribute, required. Name of the replacement glyph.

## condition
`condition` element: a single condition that has to evaluate to true for this rule to be valid.
- `tag` element: attribute, required. OpenType axis tag. 
- `operator` attribute, required. Can be either < or >
- `value` attribute, required. Number.

Rational: There are 3 ways to define a new package (see https://packaging.python.org/namespace_packages/)
1. Omit __init__.py: This solution, which is only available on Python 3.3, is equivalent to the one I have built in deploy (create_namespace). This method will manage every package in the namespace in the same way.
2. pkg_util
3. pkg_resources (setuptools): This is the method we use in openalea. The point is that method is no more recommended for distribution of new namespace, but 
    

> it’s recommended to continue using this as the different methods are not cross-compatible and  
    it’s not advisable to try to migrate an existing package.

So, for existing packages, the strategy used in openalea (pkg_resources then pkg_util) is the only one that will be cross-compatible with the existing packages

original title was: "when server is misbehaving, "wormhole send" prints errors and then hangs rather than failing"

The general fix for this is to have the client exit-with-error if the initial connection attempt fails.

#42 (which just landed) changed the client to use a `twisted.application.internet.ClientService`, which has the awesome property that it automatically reconnects to the rendezvous server if the connection is lost (e.g. when I bounce the server host while someone's `wormhole send` is running). But it applies this same level of fierce determination to the initial connection, and in our case, it would be more useful to bail and report the error to the user, because that error is probably persistent. If the first connection fails, subsequent ones probably will too.

Mypy has rules for these cases inspired by the Liskov Substitution Principle. A field's type in a subclass may be more restrictive than its type in a superclass.

So e.g. if it's a float in the superclass it could be an int in the subclass (since mypy considers int a subtype of float in this case). But switching from int to str is not allowed, because a function expecting a base class instance would access the attribute assuming it's an int, and be rudely surprised by the str from the subclass. (This is just an explanation of Liskov.)

However I think that changing some arguments to the field() call in the subclass should be fine, so the example would work.

Thanks for the PR.

The default value of `automatic_options` as written in the docstring is actually left-over from when the plugin was ported from the Flask-CORS codebase. It should not be relied to to be accurate, and I will change the docstrings in a future release.

Which value `automatic_options` should default to is something I've been thinking about for a while.

I agree setting `automatic_options` `True` is useful in many cases.
I also recognise that having it default to `False` breaks the consistency with Flask-CORS because Flask-CORS has `automatic_options` set to `True` as default behaviour, and Flask-CORS documentation is written with the assumption that `automatic_options` is turned on.

However I feel that leaving `automatic_options` default to `False` in Sanic-CORS is more consistent with the Sanic design mentality. Sanic strives to be as fast as possible out of the box. I believe Sanic plugins also need to be written to be as fast as possible with their default options.

Due to the architecture of the Sanic internal request handler, setting `automatic_options` in Sanic-CORS requires the plugin to register a request middleware function. All extra middleware functions add to the overall response time of every request. If `automatic_options` is left in its default `False` setting, then this extra request middleware is not registered.

Another argument against `automatic_options` is to look at the default accepted HTTP methods on every Sanic route. By default only `GET` is allowed, and this is 'feature' documented in the Sanic docs and well-known by Sanic users. By setting `automatic_options` to True in Sanic-CORS, this implicitly allows `OPTIONS` requests to also be accepted. If `automatic_options` is set to True in Sanic-CORS, this inconsistency with Sanic routes could cause confusion with new users. By having `automatic_options` default to `False`, the user is choosing to 'opt-out' of the default behaviour by setting it to `True`, they are indicating that they want to change the way Sanic usually works.

For these two reasons, I will keep the `automatic_options` default to False.

I will keep this PR open for comment.

@rinslow Okay, here's a decorator that returns a new copy of the original generator every time __get__ is called, using itertools.tee.

Notes:
1. Rather than storing value in self.\_\_dict__ like cached_property does, I store in a new dictionary, self.generators, as I want to fork and re-save the generator again for every new __get__ call, and don't want __dict__ to bypass it. This could probably be integrated into cached_property in a way that still keeps the __dict__ speedup for non-generators, however.
1. I have no idea if this is async-friendly.
1. I think using list() is still going to be the better approach when dealing with finite generators, in most cases.

```python
from itertools import tee

class cached_generator_property:

    def __init__(self, func):
        self.__doc__ = getattr(func, "__doc__")
        self.func = func
        self.generators = {}

    def __get__(self, obj, cls):
        if obj is None:
            return self
        if self.func.__name__ in self.generators:
            generator = self.generators[self.func.__name__]
        else:
            generator = self.func(obj)
        self.generators[self.func.__name__], value = tee(generator)
        return value


class Foo:

    @cached_generator_property
    def finite(self):
        yield from range(3)

    @cached_generator_property
    def infinite(self):
        a = 0
        while True:
            print('debug: you should only see this line once for a =', a)
            yield a
            a += 1

```

From my perspective a read-only view of the data makes a lot of sense, as opposed to allowing the user to edit and restore in the one operation, so making this major change in default behaviour in a new version of reversion sounds reasonable.

However, please don't scrap the admin inline formsets data population altogether! The inlines may be "hacky" – they actually work pretty well for us :) – but in our case an admin page without inlines would be next to useless since all the content of our CMS pages is "plugin" based and only appears in the inlines.

The strict restore feature makes it much less important for the admin page content to be entirely accurate and complete – it is enough for the admin page to show a reasonable approximation of the data that will be restored. Maybe switching to a strict restore as the default and making it clear that the data shown on admin pages (including inlines) is a best-effort only would sidestep some of the maintenance issues with the admin integration? Or at least, with users' expectations for it.

I had a look at safedelete, it should not be too hard to make it work with hvad. Maybe it could even work out of the box, I see its factory takes a `manager_superclass` and `queryset_superclass`, which should be able to build upong hvad's `TranslationManager` and `TranslationQueryset`.

It will use the default queryset class however, which means `untranslated()` would not work (it would be there, but ignore the soft deletion mechanism). I guess it would be possible to make some glue code around that.
But safedelete does not do cascade.

The other, django-permanent seems to do cascade, but it digs deeply into Django ORM internals, building queryies by manipulating and monkey-patching the internals. Trying to integrate it with any other third-party module, be it hvad or mptt or anything sounds like calling for trouble.

Depending on the scale, I believe I would just set a boolean field `is_deleted` on the models and filter on it. Basically, that's what django-safedelete does, adding some synctactic sugar on top of it. As for the cascading, I would handle it myself.

Although thinking about it, I wonder if cascading soft delete is even possible in a safe way. I mean, at the SQL level. Maybe by making the `is_deleted` field part of the foreign key, with cascading updates. But that's way out of the scope of Django ORM.

Hello,

That's interesting. Hvad only specifies the `unique_together` part and lets Django work on other relevant indexes. In particular,
 * Django should create a regular index for `master_id` automatically.
 * Hvad tells Django it wants an index of `language_code`.
 * Hvad tells Django it wants a unique_index on `[language_code, master_id]`.

On PostgreSQL, Django ends up creating exactly what's requested (two one-column indexes and the unique_index on both together, all three b-tree). Which is a bit overkill as postgresql can re-use multi-column indexes for single-column queries.

I wonder how the result you describe happens. Is it a new behavior that showed up as part of upgrading either hvad or Django? Does it do that when creating the table, or does it appear after running a migration? Which version of Django are you using?

It's your call, but I'm not sure it's that specific. As I say, this is how the django-prometheus package works.

It seems to me that there are basically 2 ways you can group request metrics, and by `request.endpoint` makes at least as much sense as `request.path`. Ultimately what you want to know with these metrics is which paths through your code are slow, so grouping by entrypoint (endpoint) seems to me exactly what most people would want in most cases (which is probably why this is what django_prometheus exposes).

I would say most applications will probably grow to the point where they are process at least hundreds of different URLs. Imagine a blog (probably the most common application for a website). Each post would have a new URL. And possible each category. Each post's page will take exactly the same path through the code, but they'll all have different URLs.

This verbosity in the output causes a significant problem - prometheus really can't easily handle parsing metrics that are thousands of lines long. It slows it down massively, and can quite easily crash it. So keeping the metrics as close to what you actually need to know is really quite important.

(I suppose you could say there is another way to group request metrics - by lumping them all in together. Some people might want to simply see the histogram of response times for all requests, not broken down at all. But maybe wait until someone asks before offering that final option.)

I agree with the importance of the test but there are two different things to test:

1. When an AirflowTaskTimeout exception is raised, does the process group for a bash task get killed?
2. Does the timeout context raise an AirflowTaskTimeout when the appropriate time has passed?

For 1, which seems to be your PR, we only need an AirflowTaskTimeout exception while the subprocess is actively running, we don't need an actual timeout.

For 2, we either need to sleep, or we need to tell the system to fire a sigalarm prematurely somehow. Either way, I think the code for checking case 2 should probably live in the tests for the timeout code, not the tests for the bash operator.

Hi @aoen my main issue is that this information should not be stored in the `task_instance` table but rather in a `task_instance_log` table. Which is your append only table. My suggestion of a primary key on `'task_id, dag_id, execution_date, start_date'` is the same as having a `try_number` in this table which you should be storing anyway.

So attempt/try_number yes, but not how it is done now. Storing it in the task_instance table doesn't make logically sense. If you want to store the count of attempt/tries it should be named `total_tries/total_attempts` if you don't want to do aggregation or a `max(start_date)`

Aha, I figured it out!  This is not a bug, although it's certainly unexpected behaviour from the user perspective, and unfortunately I can't share all the details of why it's happening because they're related to security measures.

Basically though, we do have logic that says "if you logged in from this IP within the last 24 hours, you can skip sign-in confirmation":

  * https://github.com/mozilla/fxa-auth-server/blob/69552618cf98f996d10ba399f71211059a16fbf9/lib/routes/account.js#L694

But we also have logic that says "if the request looks unusual, you're not allowed to skip sign-in confirmation":

  * https://github.com/mozilla/fxa-auth-server/blob/69552618cf98f996d10ba399f71211059a16fbf9/lib/routes/account.js#L676
  
The requests being sent by the node-js client are "more suspicious" than the ones being sent by the python client.  I can't go into why because we keep the details of what constitutes a "suspicious" request confidential, for hopefully obvious reasons.  But we could consider tweaking that filter to be more consistent here.

I'm going to close this out as it's not a bug in PyFxA, but thanks for reaching out to work through what's going on here, and I'm glad we got to the bottom of it even if I can't share all the precise details.

(And if you *do* happen to hack around with the node-js client and figure out how to make it send less "suspicious-looking" requests, please don't post the details here :-)

Tracking urls give tasks the ability to define a custom tracking url as part of the run method. The url shows up in the luigi UI. Generally this would be for a task that runs a remote job, like for hadoop, where the remote system provides some interface for tracking. We have to communicate the url up to the scheduler, and the mechanism that ended up being used for this was to piggy-back on `RemoteScheduler.add_task`, to add new info to the task.

Having update_tracking_url be nested in a method doesn't work as you found because it cannot be imported from top-level module during unpickling. Passing an instance method of `Worker` also won't work; in py 2 b/c you can't pickle instance methods, in py 2/3 because the multiprocessing objects like `Queue` must be pickled/unpickled a specific way (I think it is that they need to be instance attrs on the `Process` object).

There are a couple possible approaches, but the basic idea is that the scheduler instance needs to be pickled so that the child task process can call methods on it.  One approach is to pass the scheduler instance as an arg to `TaskProcess` init to become an attr (instead of the `tracking_url_callback` fn). Then in `TaskProcess.run()` pass a `partial(self._scheduler.add_task, ...)` to `Task.run()` or add a new instance method on `TaskProcess` and pass that.

BTW Keeping TaskProcess pickle-able may benefit more than windows users (where spawn is used), for example in linux/py3 we might want to switch to using forkserver method (avoids sharing resources from master worker process).

I'll see if I can did it out. I seem to recall that the key was to use call `fileno()` on the output and use `fcntl` to set the stdout pipe to have non-blocking reads, so that I could reliably read it until it was empty without getting stuck.

Note that an alternative option, if you have trouble getting non-blocking IO to work, is just to start a thread that reads from the `StreamingBody` and writes to the stdin pipe (and terminates when the `SteamingBody` runs dry or the far end of the pipe gets closed) and then have the main path of your code read the output from stdout and pass it down the line. In this case you need to take care to have a way to clean up the thread if the subprocess crashes.

Posting the solution here for posterity:

dd-trace-agent should work fine with Docker, but you'll have to make sure the Python side is able to connect to dd-trace-agent (by default it submits to localhost), and that the agent  accepts requests by binding to 0.0.0.0.  

We have some docs up at https://app.datadoghq.com/trace/docs/tutorials/docker

For the Python-side, the easiest way for you is probably something like
```
from ddtrace import tracer
tracer.configure(hostname="<IP address of trace-agent>")
```

You can set the agent binding via `bind_host` in `/etc/dd-agent/datadog.conf` or the DD_BIND_HOST env variable

Python Code to load YAML in OrderedDict instead of dict, taken from StackOverflow suggestion:

```python
import yaml
from collections import OrderedDict

def ordered_load(stream, Loader=yaml.Loader, object_pairs_hook=OrderedDict):
    class OrderedLoader(Loader):
        def construct_mapping(loader, node):
            loader.flatten_mapping(node)
            return object_pairs_hook(loader.construct_pairs(node))

    OrderedLoader.add_constructor(yaml.resolver.BaseResolver.DEFAULT_MAPPING_TAG, OrderedLoader.construct_mapping)
    return yaml.load(stream, OrderedLoader)


with codecs.open(file_path, mode='r', encoding='utf-8') as input:
    data = ordered_load(input)
    data = schema.validate(data)
```

The down side of two arguments is that then we lose capability to define JavaScript inline. Example this is not anymore possible.
```
| Execute JavaScript | Some code |
| ... | More code here |
| ... | ARGUMENTS |
| ... | ${variable1} |
| ... | ${variable2} |
```
Instead the code should be created as a list with a separate keyword and that is a down side. Example something like this:
```
| ${code} | Create List |Some code |
| ... | More code here |
| Execute JavaScript | ${code} |
| ... | ${variable1} |
| ... | ${variable2} |
```
In long term, this causes a big backwards compatible issue and means that it's almost impossible to to join the two keywords together. Therefore I am now against the two argument implementation.

As a second issue, we should first decide one separator and not to support multiple different separators. Like the zen of python says: There should be one -- and preferably only one -- obvious way to do it. https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/#id3

I am somewhat against the docstring, because the reasons I did explain above. Instead I would like to see something which is similar than what `Run Keyword If` uses for separating the evaluation statements. Something like `ARGUMENTS` feels natural for me, but I don't feel strongly what the actual text is.

Hi @liho26 !

The result of `deferred.addCallback` is indeed a `deferred`.

What you want is something more like this:

```python
from twisted.internet.task import react
from twisted.internet.defer import ensureDeferred
from treq import get, content

async def main():
    print(await content(await get("https://example.com/")))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    react(lambda reactor: ensureDeferred(main()))
```

I'm using `content` here rather than `json_content` since example.com doesn't produce JSON, but the concept is the same if you have a URL which does.

I hope this helps you get started using treq.  However, in the future, this is the issue tracker, for reporting bugs in Treq; if you want to ask a question, either [https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=treq&tags=twisted Stack Overflow] or [the Twisted Web mailing list](https://twistedmatrix.com/cgi-bin/mailman/listinfo/twisted-web) are more appropriate places to go for questions about how *you* are supposed to use Treq.

It's mainly to make sure that the code runs. Note that we could have a 'fast' mode for checking that it runs properly where it just runs each function once (not N times to get good stats). If the benchmark code is going to get installed for users then we have to test it.

I do get the same traceback on master. From debugging inspection, the root cause is clear:

``` python
ipdb> image.min()
0.91935977496981636
ipdb> image.max()
1.0000000000000004
```

The issue is rounding error in floating point calculations resulting in a maximum value that is _slightly_ out of range (in this case, it is 4e-15 greater than 1). 

Investigating further, the call producing this is `gaussian_filter`. The behavior appears to resolve by setting `mode='reflect'` (the default mode is `'nearest'`), but I'm unsure if this is a general solution.

Neither are encoders, per se.  `ScalarSpaceEncoder` is implemented as an encoder subclass, but its constructor returns one of `DeltaEncoder` or `AdaptiveScalarEncoder`, depending on the arguments, and never an instance of `ScalarSpaceEncoder` itself.  `PermuteEncoder` also is not an encoder, but is a `PermuteVariable` subclass used in swarming.

I don't think there's good use-case for `ScalarSpaceEncoder` as it is, since there's no advantage over calling either of the `DeltaEncoder` or `AdaptiveScalarEncoder` constructors directly.

I am pretty sure I figured out the cause. This is related to the capnp integration between nupic and nupic.bindings.

While stepping through the about-to-hang code in GDB, I noticed that the value of kj's `Mutex.futex` instance variable would change greatly and unpredictably from one invocation to the next and that the `Mutex::Mutex` constructor in nupic.bindings would never get called.

For example, on one particular invocation, I stepped into the nupic.bindings' copy of capnp here:
```
(gdb) 
capnp::SchemaLoader::loadNative (this=0x103cba0, 
    nativeSchema=0x7ffff12af820 <capnp::schemas::s_a6cdff76ea261e32>)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/ThirdParty/Source/CapnProto/src/capnp/schema-loader.c++:2068
```

with the SchemaLoader instance looking like this:
```
(gdb) p this
$17 = (capnp::SchemaLoader * const) 0x103cba0
(gdb) p *this
$18 = {impl = {mutex = {futex = 4031237736, static EXCLUSIVE_HELD = 2147483648, 
      static EXCLUSIVE_REQUESTED = 1073741824, static SHARED_COUNT_MASK = 1073741823}, value = {
      disposer = 0x7ffff07208e8, ptr = 0xfffffffffffffffd}}}

or in hex like this:
(gdb) p/x *this
$26 = {impl = {mutex = {futex = 0xf047ce68, static EXCLUSIVE_HELD = 0x80000000, 
      static EXCLUSIVE_REQUESTED = 0x40000000, static SHARED_COUNT_MASK = 0x3fffffff}, value = {
      disposer = 0x7ffff07208e8, ptr = 0xfffffffffffffffd}}}

and

(gdb) p &this->impl
$28 = (kj::MutexGuarded<kj::Own<capnp::SchemaLoader::Impl> > *) 0x103cba0
```

Right away, we notice that while the static members look correct, the instance members are completely out of whack.

Let's consider the value of futex, which is a 32-bit unsigned integer: in this futex, the upper two bits are reserved for the flags `EXCLUSIVE_HELD` and `EXCLUSIVE_REQUESTED`, and the remaining 30 bits for the shared lock count per `SHARED_COUNT_MASK`. Initially and when un-contended, the value of futex should be 0 (zero). Since the hanging test is single-threaded, the value of futex at this point should be zero, but we see 0xf047ce68 instead, which make no sense.

Next, we notice that the value of this->impl.valute.ptr is also invalid: 0xfffffffffffffffd. It should be either null or a valid pointer. 0xfffffffffffffffd is not a valid pointer.

Subsequently, upon examination of the source code involved in the call stack leading to the hang, starting with the Swig wrappers, I noticed that an instance of {{capnp::SchemaParser}} constructed by pycapnp's compilation of its own version of capnp sources was being acted upon by a method from nupic.bindings' compilation of nupic.bindings' own copy of capnp.

When installed on Ubuntu 16.04, pycapnp's capnp sources are compiled by gcc/g++ 5.4.0, while the capnp sources belonging to nupic.bindings were compiled under CentOS6.8 using gcc/g++ 4.8.2. Besides a major version difference in the toolchain, they were also almost certainly compiled using different compilation option. Together, this would very likely result in code generation and object layout differences.

This also likely sheds light into why the tests passed on the Ubuntu 14.04 installation, in which gcc/g++ 4.8.4 were used to compile pycapnp's capnp sources, which is a lot closer to the 4.8.2 toolchain that is used to generate nupic.bindings.

THE DETAILS
One hanging call stack looks like this:
{code}
Program received signal SIGINT, Interrupt.
syscall () at ../sysdeps/unix/sysv/linux/x86_64/syscall.S:38
38	../sysdeps/unix/sysv/linux/x86_64/syscall.S: No such file or directory.
(gdb) bt
#0  syscall () at ../sysdeps/unix/sysv/linux/x86_64/syscall.S:38
#1  0x00007ffff0f49d57 in kj::_::Mutex::lock (this=0x1046560, exclusivity=<optimized out>)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/ThirdParty/Source/CapnProto/src/kj/mutex.c++:91
#2  0x00007ffff0f19c5e in kj::MutexGuarded<kj::Own<capnp::SchemaLoader::Impl> >::lockExclusive (
    this=0x1046560) at /nupic.core/build/scripts/ThirdParty/Source/CapnProto/src/kj/mutex.h:300
q#3  capnp::SchemaLoader::loadNative (this=0x1046560, 
    nativeSchema=0x7ffff12af820 <capnp::schemas::s_a6cdff76ea261e32>)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/ThirdParty/Source/CapnProto/src/capnp/schema-loader.c++:2069
#4  0x00007ffff0d47bc3 in capnp::SchemaLoader::loadCompiledTypeAndDependencies<RandomProto> (
    this=<optimized out>)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/ThirdParty/Install/include/capnp/schema-loader.h:168
#5  capnp::SchemaParser::loadCompiledTypeAndDependencies<RandomProto> (this=<optimized out>)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/ThirdParty/Install/include/capnp/schema-parser.h:83
#6  nupic::getBuilder<RandomProto> (pyBuilder=0x7fffef77ec80)
    at /nupic.core/src/nupic/py_support/PyCapnp.hpp:77
#7  0x00007ffff0b73183 in nupic_Random_write__SWIG_2 (self=0x10c5710, pyBuilder=<optimized out>)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/src/nupic/bindings/mathPYTHON_wrap.cxx:9209
#8  0x00007ffff0b76ecb in _wrap_Random_write__SWIG_2 (nobjs=nobjs@entry=2, 
    swig_obj=swig_obj@entry=0x7fffffffb840)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/src/nupic/bindings/mathPYTHON_wrap.cxx:136466
#9  0x00007ffff0b7733d in _wrap_Random_write (self=0x0, args=<optimized out>)
    at /nupic.core/build/scripts/src/nupic/bindings/mathPYTHON_wrap.cxx:136588
#10 0x00000000004cb26d in PyEval_EvalFrameEx ()
{code}

Note that {{nupic::getBuilder<RandomProto>}} implementation (compiled into nupic.bindings' _math.so) looks like this:
{code}
  template<class T>
  typename T::Builder getBuilder(PyObject* pyBuilder)
  {
    PyObject* capnpModule = PyImport_AddModule("capnp.lib.capnp");
    PyObject* pySchemaParser = PyObject_GetAttrString(capnpModule,
                                                      "_global_schema_parser");
    pycapnp_SchemaParser* schemaParser = (pycapnp_SchemaParser*)pySchemaParser;
    schemaParser->thisptr->loadCompiledTypeAndDependencies<T>();

    pycapnp_DynamicStructBuilder* dynamicStruct =
        (pycapnp_DynamicStructBuilder*)pyBuilder;
    capnp::DynamicStruct::Builder& builder = dynamicStruct->thisptr;
    typename T::Builder proto = builder.as<T>();
    return proto;
  }
{code}

The code above gets {{_global_schema_parser}} from pycapnp in python, then casts it to {{pycapnp_SchemaParser*}}, and invokes {{loadCompiledTypeAndDependencies<T>()}} on its {{thisptr}} member, which is a pointer to the {{SchemaParser}} instance instantiated by capnp sources compiled into the pycapnp python extension. I think that the code writer's expectation was that {{loadCompiledTypeAndDependencies<T>()}} would call into pycapnp's python extension. However, because {{nupic::getBuilder<RandomProto>}} is compiled into nupic.bindings' python extension that includes its own version/copy of capnp sources, the call to {{loadCompiledTypeAndDependencies<T>()}} resolved against capnp in {{_math.so}} during _math.so linking, instead. And this was its undoing.

>  Makefiles are indeed out of context in a Python project.

Please note we are talking *example app instructions* here, and these are *shell commands*, such as `flask initdb`. They are not Python commands. Makefile is a perfectly usable tool for automatising running shell commands of that sort.

If you want to see some use case examples in Python projects:
- what I have in mind is akin to Sphinx's use of Makefiles, that we *already have* in each Invenio package anyway: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/docs/Makefile
- and I gave an example of pypa's warehouse before, to cite just one more example: https://github.com/pypa/warehouse/blob/master/Makefile

Let me try with a story, perhaps it can illustrate my thoughts better. Let us imagine that we have now introduced `README.rst` or `README.sh`, which gives:

```
README.sh
app-fixtures.sh
app.py
app-setup.sh
app-teardown.sh
data
requirements.txt
```

I would argue that we have an unnecessary clutter here, because the example app is small, and the helper shell scripts like `app-teardown.sh` are tiny, typically 1-2 liners only, such as: *(illustrative example only)*

```console
$ cat app-teardown.sh
flask db drop
```

Say I don't like the additional clutter that this brings to the source code file tree. Wouldn't people be able to focus better on the example app without seeing all that additional clutter?

One way to address the clutter problem would be to "hide" these ultra tiny helper shell scripts inside `README.sh` as shell functions, for example:

```console
$ cat README.sh

setup () {
   flask init
   flask create
}

populate () {
   flask fixtures records
}

teardown () {
   flask db drop
}

if [ "$1" = "setup" ]; then
    setup
elif [ "$1" = "populate" ]; then
    populate
else
  ...

```

This would give nicely uncluttered source tree:

```
README.sh
app.py
data
requirements.txt
```

and people would type:

```console
$ ./README.sh setup
$ ./README.sh populate
$ firefox http://0.0.0.0/records/1
$ ./README.sh teardown
```

to run the example app.  (If `README.sh` looks a bit weird here, let's imagine `app.sh` or `run.sh` or something; the exact name of the unified helper script is of secondary importance to the story.)

So far so good; the clutter is gone, the focus is back.

However, let's ponder how is this different from the Makefile approach? With a Makefile, we'd have similar content (with less boiler plate):

```console`
$ cat Makefile

setup:
   flask init
   flask create

populate:
   flask fixtures records

teardown:
   flask db drop
```

with similarly uncluttered files:

```
Makefile
app.py
data
requirements.txt
```

with people typing similarly:

```console
$ make setup
$ make populate
$ firefox http://0.0.0.0/records/1
$ make teardown
```

to run this example app.

The two examples are pretty similar, aren't they? In other words, aren't we simply *"rediscovering"* the standard Makefile facility, if we start from the `README.sh` approach first, using a plethora of tiny helper scripts, and if we try to unclutter the source code tree to hide away those unnecessary tiny helper scripts later?

(Moreover, let's imagine the additional goodies that using the standard Makefile tool would bring. For example, people could type `make <TAB> <RET>` to see all the available options, with auto-completed value of either `setup`, `populate` or `teardown` for them, thanks to the usual Makefile shell completion. Yes, we could write a dedicated shell completion snippet for `README.sh` too, but isn't this again a sign that illustrates my point about "reinventing" the Makefile wheel again?)

In my eyes, this use of Makefiles is pretty common in the Unix world. Makefile is useful not only to compile C programs and the like.  See the Sphinx example, see various TeX examples... I would guess that most developers had come across examples of this kind during their exposure to Unix.

I agree that the correct solution needs to implement a hash based on an immutable subset of the equality criteria implemented by __eq__, which would basically boil down to a hash that depends only on the field type. (Equality of two fields requires their types to be the same plus most field properties according to the field's own schema to be equal. These properties can be changed after the fact, so the immutable part of the criteria is the set of property names, which in turn depends on the field type.)

But such a hash would mean a backwards-incompatible change: for example, code that uses fields as dict keys may rely on being able to use fields from different schemas that are not identical but compare as equal otherwise. Therefore, I suggest going with the status quo for now, i.e. using id(self), and switching to hash(type(self)) for the next major release of zope.schema.

> I wonder if it is such a good idea to keep geopy's `exactly_one` parameter *and* HERE's `maxresults` one?

Yep, both should be present. See this comment for rationale and precedence explanation: https://github.com/geopy/geopy/pull/281#discussion_r180079906

> In fact, for `maxresults` to make most sense I would need to have a `pageinformation` param for implementing some simple paging mechanism, too. But I can't find anything on "paging" in this package. Has really no other plugin implemented paging?

I'd suggest to not bother about pagination. Looping through result pages is very rarely (if ever) needed for geocoding results. Usually blindly selecting the first result (i.e. with `exactly_one=True` by default) is good enough.

`maxresults` is perfectly viable even without `pageinformation`. For example, to limit the default page size (what is it, ~20?) to 5, which might be reasonable for live-search suggestions.

Hmm, there's no mechanism for embedding a widget in a qgrid cell currently, but I *think* you should be able to accomplish what you want by adding a couple of columns and double clicking to edit their values instead of clicking a button.

In your case it sounds like maybe you'd want a categorical column to use for the positive/negative flag, with 3 possible values: `positive`, `negative`, and the empty string `""` to represent the unset state. For the "unimportant" flag, you could use a boolean column that defaults to `False`, and then set a filter on the column to make the `True` rows disappear (so when you check the box for the cell, the row disappears).  Alternatively you could use the "Remove Row" button to remove the row entirely but it sounds like hiding the row via a flag is what you're going for.

Here's some sample code which includes "result" and "unimportant" columns to show you what I mean in the above description:
```df_types = pd.DataFrame({
    'A' : 1.,
    'B' : pd.Series(['2013-01-01', '2013-01-02', '2013-01-03', '2013-01-04',
               '2013-01-05', '2013-01-06', '2013-01-07', '2013-01-08', '2013-01-09'],index=list(range(9)),dtype='datetime64[ns]'),
    'C' : pd.Series(randn(9),index=list(range(9)),dtype='float32'),
    'D' : np.array([3] * 9,dtype='int32'),
    'result' : pd.Categorical(["", "", "", "", "","", "", "", ""], categories=["","positive","negative"]),
 })
df_types['unimportant'] = False
qgrid_widget = qgrid.QgridWidget(df=df_types, show_toolbar=True)
qgrid_widget
```

Another possibility would be to have an ipywidget button above or below the grid rather than one for each row, and make it so the button click affects the row(s) that are currently selected in the grid (you can multi-select by shift-clicking).  In other words, the workflow becomes two-click instead of one (i.e. one click to select the rows to be marked, then another click of an ipywidget button to take the action on the selected rows).  This would require using undocumented aspects of qgrid's API and might require some small code changes so it's probably not worth pursuing if the simpler "add columns" solution described above works for you.

I do have a client that wants this additional layer of split brain protection. I have deployed watchdog and SBD based clusters with Pacemaker and actually found it much more robust than STONITH based approaches.

I'm currently proceeding with the integrated approach. The main benefits of the external watchdog don't matter much in my opinion:

* The external watchdog can monitor various system parameters like running out of memory or too high load. In my experience the cause for such issues is more often than not system independent, in which case doing a hard failover only makes things worse.
* The external watchdog can try to repair the system by e.g. restarting Patroni. But I'm not sure how many failovers will this prevent. I think doing our best to not crash, and rebooting the host when we do is sufficient.

I'm coding the watchdog module to support platform dependent implementations. For now I'm only doing Linux, but BSD support would look pretty much the same, only with different ioctl values for the operations. I'm sure Windows and other platforms could also be supported if someone really has enough interest to look up the documentation for it.

I'm kind of undecided whether the default for the feature should be off or automatic. Automatic meaning that it will try to use the watchdog interface if it's available and it has permissions for it. In that case enabling watchdog support would be as easy as `modprobe softdog && chown postgres /dev/watchdog`.

I will also make the watchdog support cluster aware. I want the watchdog to be disabled when only one instance remains in the cluster. If you do run into a situation where watchdog triggers due to high load the last thing you want is to kill the last remaining instance. Probably will also want to add an exponential backoff to the TTL timeout to avoid failover ping-pong failure mode. But this looks like a separate feature.

Basically the idea is to make Patroni not do too much damage in cases where competent DBA response time can be multiple hours.

Hi Paul,

Adding support for ORing between conditions is one of my TODO items... My plan was to make it look like Django's Q objects, where you can do things like:
```
conditions = Q(first_name='bob', last_name='smith') | Q(name='mary', last_name='jones')
Person.objects.filter(conditions)
```
I think this should be possible by enhancing our Q class:
- `__init__`: add self._mode which can be 'AND' or 'OR'
- implement `__or__` and `__and__` methods, which provide the "|" and "&" operators between Q objects. So q1|q2 should return a new Q object joining the conditions from q1 and q2 with an OR operator. That's the trickiest part, I guess.
- `to_sql`: when the Q object is made up of child Q objects, it should join their SQL using self._mode - something like `"({}) {} ({})".format(q1.to_sql(model_cls), self._mode, q2.to_sql(model_cls)`.

So if you want to take a crack at this and create a pull request, you're most welcome to do so. If not, I'll try to get to it soon.

Good call. Patching `requests.session.Session.send` works better in this case. There are three things you probably most care about regarding talking to DynamoDB: 1: network roundtrip time, 2: return status code 3: internal retries.

For current patching you only get 1. But patching the botocore vendored requests you get all three benefits. Though it means a little bit more work on parameter capture since you go one level deeper on the code path. 

The X-Ray SDK support requests only if your application has direct dependency on it. It leaves botocore vendored requests untouched. So when you patch PynamoDB you can directly dive into the vendored requests and do the necessary patch there. You just need to make sure when you have for example 2 retries, it creates 3 sibling subsegments (1 original call + 2 retries) where for each subsegment it represents a DynamoDB call. This way you gain more value on tracing the DynamoDB calls from your contribution.

The AWS Go SDK has hooks that provide insights on internal retries and TLS info for each retry. Check what you can get from X-Ray Go SDK https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-go. Although boto doesn't provide as much as Go you can still get more insights on why a call is taking long.

I've got a bead on some code that will address the 2D despeckling issue. It uses ndimage.label and accounts for the periodic boundary issue in 360 PPIs, which is easier to solve than you might think. Basically, append a sweep to itself, label objects, relabel the objects along the right edge of the first sweep copy with adjacent values on the left edge, then remove the duplicate sweep. Here is an example that shows deletion of additional pixels that were missed during the 1D despeckling approach in CSU_RadarTools. Note that user can specify maximum size to consider a speckle. 

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3036387/15381988/9461edf4-1d4a-11e6-9fe3-afa5ef46ee67.png)

The great part is that you can also use the engine to find cells in sweeps, and port the labels to the radar object as a separate field. You can even apply a smoother first to avoid over-diagnosing objects. Here are objects greater than 40 dBZ in the scan from above:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3036387/15382063/7d1ae3de-1d4b-11e6-8ff1-18fc3953af53.png)

Checking a few version of numpy with conda, I found that NumPy 1.9 is required for the unit tests to pass, but the failures from older versions (1.8 and 1.7) are likely caused by test which expect an over ambitious precision and an minor API change in matplotlib which I doubt would effect actual use of the package.  NumPy 1.6 fails a few more tests but against mostly due to minor precision issues and a few API changes. 

Is it worth putting in the time to track down and fix any bugs so that exact minimum version of Py-ART dependencies can be specified?  It definitely could be done, and a Travis job could be added to run tests in this minimum environment, but I'm not sure if the payoff would be worth the effort.

I'll weigh in with my two cents. For a lot of workflows, keeping the file open can quickly become burdensome(I often find myself touching 1K+ files). Also, I personally prefer for the readers to be more predictable. I prefer to know when I'll incur the io/time hit for reading a file rather than lazy evaluation, unless it is optional(I can see good use cases for both). The question then becomes, if it is optional, does it add a significant maintenance burden to keep two different paths in the codebase? 

As to replacing the fields with classes, I'm personally against it, though for reasons that may be more laziness than technical. In particular, right now it is easy to use savez and savemat to move data back and forth between different scripts and matlab/IDL(By just saving out the entire fields dict). If we move to classes, I imagine that would become significantly more challenging. 

Also, while I fully support the option to only load certain variables for memory reasons, as Jonathan pointed out, it will not provide any(or only very minimal) actual loading or processing time benefits for the vast majority of the radar formats due to how they interleave the moments.

It is impossible to autohint a font with flex hints in FontLab with Autohint.py with the same result as from the command line, because FontLab is obviously unable to store the flex hints, that Autohint.py creates

And some of the regular hints, that autohintexe creates, can be different, if you suppress flex hints. If autohintexe runs with flex hints activated, it seems to prepare some of the regular hints for the flex hints.

In my opinion the macro is only useful, if your font shall not contain flex hints. Otherwise it is better to autohint the font from the command line.

And instinctivly I would say, that, if you want to generate a font in FontLab with flex hints and the autohint macro, you should let run the modified macro with no-flex option activated. And the you let FontLab create the flex hints.

But because you cannot turn of flex hint generation in the original macro dialog, I propose to replace the original Autohint.py and BezChar.py with the files, in which I have added the no-flex option.

AFAICT you only use `type` as a positional argument in `MetricDescriptor.__init__` and `ResourceDescriptor.__init__`. So the name is somewhat less important since positional. Though maybe that isn't true for you, I noticed most calls to these constructors uses all keyword args.

As for setting `self.type = type_`, that isn't problematic since `self.type` is on the objects namespace, which cascades through `self.__dict__`, `type(self).__dict__`, and then down the MRO of the parent class. Thus there is no clobbering of `__builtins__` when a builtin name is used as an instance / class property.

Are you still opposed to changing `type` -> `type_` given this distinction between positional argument and instance attribute?

@Preston-Landers Thank you for your interest in the library. Currently, there is no support for v2 endpoint in ADAL Python as you have already discovered. We are evaluating plans for providing client library support for v2 endpoint and will update once they are concrete.

This is an excellent question! After some research, I believe the current ADAL Python implementation is close enough. Because:

* The standard [OAuth2 protocol defines "expires_in" only](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.1.4), there is no "expires_on", so a client is supposed to calculate the expiry time locally.
* [Microsoft Azure Active Directory returns an "expires_on" field](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/v1-protocols-oauth-code#successful-response-1) as an extra information.
* In practice, the difference-in-seconds between the above two, does not matter. Because later when [ADAL Python checks for token expiry](https://github.com/AzureAD/azure-activedirectory-library-for-python/blob/1.0.2/adal/cache_driver.py#L170-L171), we will add some minutes-long buffer.

This snippet would never have worked as far as I know. You're calling `._tr` on the return value of Row.cells, which is a tuple of Cell objects, hence the `AttributeError`:

```python
hdr_cells = table.rows[0].cells
print hdr_cells
hdr_cells[0].text = title
set_merge(hdr_cells[0], nCols)
for ki in range(nCols-1):
    if len(hdr_cells) > 1:
        n = hdr_cells.__getitem__(1)._tc
        if n is not None:
            hdr_cells._tr.remove(n)
```

I suspect what you're looking for is this:

```python
...
if len(hdr_cells) > 1:
    tc = hdr_cells.__getitem__(1)._tc
    if tc is not None:
        tc._tr.remove(tc)
```

But note that cell merging was added a couple releases back and that will take care of all that bit for you.
http://python-docx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/table.html#docx.table._Cell.merge

Your second example can be implemented like this:

```python
def set_row_height(row, height):
    trPr = row.tr.get_or_add_trPr()
    trHeight = OxmlElement('w:trHeight')
    trHeight.set(qn('w:val'), str(height))
    trPr.append(trHeight)
```

Note that this, like many workaround functions, is only likely to work on newly-added tables. If the `w:trPr` element already has children, the `w:trHeight` element is likely to appear out of order by adding it at the end with `.append()`; order of child elements is significant in WordprocessingML.

The output of the lookup transform have to be one-dimensional (ie. scalar). So, instead of a single lookup operation that returns a two-value tuple, you would need to have two lookup operations that return a single value each.

>  1. Would it be possible to store the table outside the class in case the file is very large or in case it changes?

I don't think that the size of the `base_dict` argument (to the constructor of `LookupTransformer` dictionary) affects the save in pickle data format (and the subsequent conversion to PMML) in any way. 

But we will know for sure when we have the first prototype ready.

> 2. We would like to minimize space and hopefully O(1) lookup.

The JPMML-Evaluator library implements O(1) lookup, so 33k row table won't be a problem.

I do like the focus that usaddress has on parsing; normalization, disambiguation and standardization are often handled as different steps.

Normalization is pretty simple, using a dictionary for most cases:
https://github.com/derrek/street-address/blob/master/lib/street_address.rb

It could be a little more complex, if you want to handle edit distance.. (e.g. "Nrth").

Disambiguation and standardization often take actual data from the USPS or from the Tiger census (for US addresses), to cover the corner cases.
