A few points here:

1) I could certainly not recommend an IP-based whitelisting because it is not feasible in situations when process runs in containers or on a remote server, while it also forces very strong restriction of "from where one can access certain features rather than who can access them"
2) We must segregate administrative features from informational stuff
3) I think "HTTP Basic Authentication" would serve the purpose and implementing that would be very simple in Flask (default credentials could be `admin/admin`, but modifiable using a CLI argument and an environment variable when the replay server instance is run). This approach would not require a full-blown authentication system with a database, while authenticated session automatically last quite long from the same browser

basic IPLD support has been merged into master, and will be released in 0.4.5 (likely before the end of the year). While the tooling around it so far is a bit sparse (we're still figuring out the best interfaces for this), you can use it and resolve things through it already. For example, take this ipld object (written in JSON):

```json
{
        "Hello": "World",
        "cats": {
                "kitty": {"/": "QmZ2MNo4QesxepKgYiFSaHDDYa9wuETKDRW2pPm7Fu6rsp"},
                "moustache": {"/": "QmPPD8EeDsXJVoWEHHWBY2QRB8sejcxanVeqCybstpLkMY"},
                "bulletcat": {"/": "QmS2rX3vcFJdaZxn88rFoknKcLavxbX4kok7xoFfnirSTT"}
        },
        "code": {"/": "QmTG7sMBQXy6niraGSL9mjWvHUHJaMQYKAJ9Stqwmzo992"},
        "catArray": [
                {"/": "QmZ2MNo4QesxepKgYiFSaHDDYa9wuETKDRW2pPm7Fu6rsp"},
                {"/": "QmPPD8EeDsXJVoWEHHWBY2QRB8sejcxanVeqCybstpLkMY"},
                {"/": "QmS2rX3vcFJdaZxn88rFoknKcLavxbX4kok7xoFfnirSTT"}
        ]
}
```

You can put this into ipfs like:

```
> cat foo.json | ipfs dag put
zdpuAxSForPxtBPWPQRfnkMvq5W7ooT9uN9xNutvSqypSmToY
```

And then view paths over this like:
- https://ipfs.io/ipfs/zdpuAxSForPxtBPWPQRfnkMvq5W7ooT9uN9xNutvSqypSmToY/code
- https://ipfs.io/ipfs/zdpuAxSForPxtBPWPQRfnkMvq5W7ooT9uN9xNutvSqypSmToY/cats/moustache
- https://ipfs.io/ipfs/zdpuAxSForPxtBPWPQRfnkMvq5W7ooT9uN9xNutvSqypSmToY/catArray/2

Please Note that we didn't implemented https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-idr-flowspec-redirect-ip-02 yet (as was discussed in https://github.com/Exa-Networks/exabgp/issues/306)

We only implement the first draft 
https://tools.ietf.org/rfcdiff?url2=draft-ietf-idr-flowspec-redirect-ip-01.txt

I am unlikely to update before an RFC is released as I would break some installation.

The code says :

```
def redirect (tokeniser):
	data = tokeniser()
	if data.count(':') == 1:
		prefix,suffix = data.split(':',1)
		if prefix.count('.'):
			raise ValueError('this format has been deprecaded as it does not make sense and it is not supported by other vendors')

		asn = int(prefix)
		route_target = int(suffix)
```

So it looks I do not accept IP notation in the first part, please use an INT and see if it helps.

Hello Pavel, This is because on shutdown BGP does not send the the withdrawn routes, so there is nothing to send. It just send a notification. So you you make sure you keep a copy of the routes you installed and in the process section register for "neighbour-changes" and on shutdown or peer down, you should remove the routes you added.

Wait!  `{'network_disabled' : False}` **did** fix my problem!  But it was masked by another important problem that might be related the source of the bug.  In my code I iterate until the container disappears from the list of running containers:

```
while container['Id'] in [i['Id'] for i in c.containers()]:
        print c.stats(container['Id'], stream=False)
        time.sleep(2)
```

With `{'network_disabled' : False}` that loops correctly _until the container is no longer running_.  Then, and _I suspect there's a race condition here_, what should be the final call to `stats()` hangs.  My solution is to set the client timeout to something smaller and less annoying like 15 seconds and do this:

```
    while container['Id'] in [i['Id'] for i in c.containers()]:
        try:
            stats = c.stats(container['Id'], stream=False)
        except:
            break
        print stats
        time.sleep(2)
```

Thanks for the clarification.
The instance_ufo are being saved by MutatorMath, via defcon, via ufoLib.
Ultimately, ufoLib UFOWriter (called by defcon.Font.save()) should remove any lingering .glif files if they are no longer referenced in the contents.plist. If this doesn't happen when saving to the same location as a pre-existing UFO, then I'd say this is bug.
The glyphOrder does not have anything to do with "what glyph is present?", just with "in which order should I compile (or display) the glyphs that are actually present?". At least, in theory.

Thanks all for the feedbacks and instructions!

Here is a brief summary of where we are currently:

1. Not all of these Filters are supported yet. At least **Transforms** and **RoundCorner** seems to work. Be careful about the unclear syntax in customParameter. - punchcutter 
2. Keep the plugin/module structure(**What does it mean?**). Be careful about supporting 'include|exclude:list, of, glyph, names' as last argument. - schriftgestalt 
3. Implement Filter feature in ufo2ft lib so that both glyphsLib, ufo and other apps can use it.  - anthrotype 
4. Transform the alignment zooms when generating **hinted OTFs** - JelleBosmaMT  
5. Make everything in glyphsLib class based. - jamesgk
6. Cosimo and Georg will [talk about](https://github.com/googlei18n/glyphsLib/issues/136#issuecomment-287904175) the class branch in Berlin. 

Plug-in/class based approach is still under discussion between Cosimo and Georg so I'm focusing on a straightforward fix for this issue for now. (And @marekjez86 is also waiting for this to be fix ASAP.)

I like the idea that this filter could be implemented in ufo2ft so that other applications can also use it. 

My concern is that the syntax of 'Filter' is defined in glyphsApp manual therefore glyphsLib should be responsible to handle it. (IMHO, the goal of glyphsLib is to perform a lossless conversion from .glyphs to UFOs and .designspace (Is it possible?)) The glyphsLib will depend on the correctness of ufo2ft if we let ufo2ft handle the glyphsApp functionality. And it sort of breaks the modularization I guess.

Alternatively, if we really want to make some filter/custom parameters globally usable, we define ufo2ft's own filter specification, so that every app that want to use ufo2ft to compile the font must respect ufo2ft specification and provide ufo2ft recognizable ufo files. 

As @punchcutter pointed out, offsetX and offsetY is fairly simple in defcon:

```
for glyph in ufo:
    glyph.move((0, -51))
```

I think let glyphsLibs to handle it is straightforward from my understanding. But all other comments are welcome.

The lower case `dflt` is a language code, while the upper case `DFLT` is script code. The case usually matters here, though in this particular case it is a matter convention since `DFLT` script is just a syntactic sugar and not actually written to the font as such.

I guess it doesn't make any sense for MutatorMath to calculate negative angles, assuming none of the masters have negative angles (which they shouldn't, since they are validated and saved successfully before interpolation). So I guess the correct fix is something in MutatorMath.

import allennlp
import os
import sys
import argparse

from allennlp.commands.predict import predict
from allennlp.commands import main



curr_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
model_dir = os.path.join(curr_dir, "models")
srl_model_file = os.path.join(model_dir, "srl_model.tar.gz")
output_file = os.path.join(model_dir, "output.txt")
input_file = os.path.join(model_dir, "input.txt")

def predict():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()

    parser.add_argument("archive_file", type=str, help="Model for SRL", default=srl_model_file)
    parser.add_argument("input_file", metavar="input-file", type=argparse.FileType('r'),
                        help="Path to input file.", default=input_file)
    parser.add_argument("--output-file", type=argparse.FileType('w'), help="Path to output file.", default=output_file)
    parser.set_defaults(func=predict)

    args = parser.parse_args()

    print(args)

    args.func(args)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    predict()

Having EOS appended to the source gives decoder something it can always attend to while generating target EOS.  Without source EOS, it mostly learns to attend to the last source token whatever it is (e.g. dot, question mark, or some word). This might require more effort from the model then just explicitly attending to the source EOS in all cases. E.g. my recent model (not exactly seq2seq, but anyway), at its first epochs, does not generate EOS when I remove the dot from the end of the source.

I was a little worried when the installation didn't show any progress for over ten minutes after printing progress to the console every second but it finally worked out.

@kalefranz out of curiosity I rebased this to see what it would achieve. This currently fails a conda list test, but no others---and it seems to offer a lot of savings (like 25%, maybe). Python 2.7 finishes in under 20 minutes. 

Might be worth fixing `conda list --json` to make this work. I know why it's happening---because `conda list --json` just dumps the json representation of Dist, which now has a lot fewer fields. Dump a subset of the corresponding IndexRecord instead and you're golden.

To be clear, I don't think we should ever promise `--clone` to work for packages whose source no longer exists. So for example, if you build a package locally, install it, and then _remove_ the package from your build directory, `--clone` may eventually fail.

I say "eventually" because when a local package is installed, the tarball is copied into the package cache and tagged with its `file://` URL. As long as that package cache entry still exists, it should still be cloneable. But if anything happens to the cache, and it needs to retrieve that tarball again, it will expect it to exist in its original location.

I don't really understand your comment. Checking out a tag is effectively the same a checking out a commit - it's a fixed reference to a point in time in the repository, and it doesn't matter what the history is before (or after) that tag/commit. The cached download would be incomplete if you built against a *different* tag, yes. But then you'd only need to download the shallow version of that tag (which you would have to do anyway, with a non-shallow clone, and it might be smaller (if not much has changed), or it might be bigger (if more data in the intervening history than in the single commit)). Basically the same as having to download a new .tar.gz from pypi or whatever.

In any case, I don't mind if it's *not* the default, but it would still be great to have the option. I'm on a poor (and expensive) connection, and downloading a new 2Mb shallow copy of the latest version of pylearn2 every time I update is a *lot* less pain than trying to download the whole 200Mb repository history, only to have it fail at 150Mb multiple times...

Thanks for the info.

Yeah WebAssembly seems potentially very interesting in the sciences. We have some cases where we share HTML documents of smallish analyzed data. Users like these as a way to explore the results easily and gain insight, but constantly want to add new features to them, which can be difficult to keep up with and wrangle into a standard interface. At some point, it might make more sense to embed a Python interpreter with some dependencies and hand this off instead. Certainly one can imagine other ways to do this Conda, Docker, etc., but that requires the user be able to handle some install step on their own, which is not always guaranteed (especially if there are some rough edges). Also there is a question of how to version the data and the tools together. Things like Pyodide look really attractive for solving these kinds of problems.

> I don't know enough about conda-build yet. Is it critical that conda-build itself builds the build dependencies, or would it be enough to use an emsdk already present?

Am still trying to figuring things out in this space myself. Hopefully we can fill in the relative gaps in each others' understanding through this discussion. 😄

Just recently was able to build the LLVM stack with WebAssembly support and compile some basic programs. ( https://github.com/conda-forge/llvmdev-feedstock/pull/42 ) Also have been able to build some things like [Binaryen]( https://github.com/conda-forge/binaryen-feedstock ). So maybe this is a reasonable path. It's interesting that you mention a self-hosting compiler. Have you seen [cib]( https://github.com/tbfleming/cib )? Maybe that is another path to consider. [Discussion with Emscripten developers]( https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/issues/6952 ) should help.

In any event, what we will want to figure out is [what is a compiler for WebAssembly]( https://conda.io/docs/user-guide/tasks/build-packages/compiler-tools.html#customizing-the-compilers ). This may be Fastcomp + Emscripten, LLVM/Clang + Emscripten or emsdk, but we will want to codify that at some point and explain what an activated compiler looks like (e.g. what environment variables do we set, how do we configure it, etc.). We can also support multiple options this way if needed.

> I'm not aware of another toolchain that addresses the problem of porting legacy C and C++ to WebAssembly as completely as emscripten. Most of the others are only appropriate if you were starting today and targetting WebAssembly, and don't need a filesystem or the cstdlib, etc.

This is a really good point. The [WebAssembly libc]( https://github.com/jfbastien/musl ) solution looks to be still very young, but expect that will change. Looks like there is a lot of work in that direction. Agree that this sort of functionality probably needs to be coming from Emscripten to start.

At this point am more thinking of the backend. IOW can we get away with vanilla LLVM + experimental WASM support as the backend (Emscripten as the front end). Thus far it's not clear to me the answer is no. IIUC that is what Rust does now. ( https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/44006 )

> Dynamic linking is supported, and dlopen works basically as you would expect when using emscripten, so it is possible to load .so files (including Python extensions) at runtime. Is there some JavaScript shim code that is handling the loading bits?

This is very good to hear. Makes the package management problem much more tractable.

> There are some problems to solve on Chrome given its requirement for asynchronous compilation...

Guess you are referring to issue ( https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/issues/6633 )?

> Emscripten allows filesystems to be overlayed...

Interesting. So how are these packages hosted? What does the format look like? How are versions tracked?

> A good strategy to declare dependencies would probably be to model it after conda's dependency solver. We might want to port the important bits of the conda client to Javascript or Rust/C/Wasm to make this work.

At one point there was [libconda]( https://github.com/conda/libconda ). This has been basically abandoned AFAIK, but there have occasionally been use cases for reviving it. The SAT solver is in [pycosat]( https://github.com/ContinuumIO/pycosat ), which might be a fun thing to build with WebAssembly. Though if NumPy is doable, it shouldn't be too hard.

Maybe this would be a good place to start? @kalefranz thoughts on this?

I'm sorry you're having a bad time with Curator.  _You're having a bad experience, **and it's my goal to fix it.**_  From what I can tell, though, it can only be indirectly Curator's fault from what the errors are indicating.

## Flow

Let's look at the flow again: 

* Curator creates a client connection to Elasticsearch using the elasticsearch-py module
* The command to delete a snapshot is issued to Elasticsearch using elasticsearch-py calls
* Elasticsearch, via the Amazon S3 plugin, issues the call to Amazon
* The response you're getting is coming from Amazon, back to Elasticsearch, back to elasticsearch-py, up the chain to Curator again.

What this means is that the error:

```
elasticsearch.exceptions.NotFoundError: TransportError(404, u'RemoteTransportException[[Lemuel Dorcas][inet[/10.11.102.53:9300]][cluster/snapshot/delete]]; nested: SnapshotMissingException[[my_s3_repository:curator-20150213001500] is missing]; nested: FileNotFoundException[The specified key does not exist. (Service: Amazon S3; Status Code: 404; Error Code: NoSuchKey; Request ID: 5531B70DBC6BD25B)]; ')
```

...is, at its root, coming from Elasticsearch.  You should actually be able to find a corresponding error in your Elasticsearch log files (in fact, I'd love to see them inline in a response here).  Neither Curator nor the elasticsearch-py module have any ability of themselves to connect to S3, which is handled by Elasticsearch and the S3 plugin.  This is why I was suggesting a client update on that end might be a potential fix.  Can you tell me what version of the S3 plugin you are using in your Elasticsearch? And with which Elasticsearch version?

## Possible causes

* **The calls to delete are happening too quickly in succession**
This is, at best, a guess.  Elasticsearch is, indeed, unable to find the file.  There could be no 404 otherwise.  Amazon is sending the 404—`(Service: Amazon S3; Status Code: 404; Error Code: NoSuchKey; Request ID: 5531B70DBC6BD25B)]; ')`—complete with a request ID.  Why Amazon would do this, I can only speculate.  **My guess is that it's an incorrect 404** because too many calls are hitting at a time, and it's not able to search the file-system quickly enough so the request actually results in a timeout of some kind on their end.  That's why you can still find it later with a curl call (things aren't hammering the server).

* **There is some kind of background retry logic happening in the S3 plugin**
This ties in to the "too many requests in rapid succession" case above.  The call may go through to delete, but Amazon (or Elasticsearch) issue their own retry.  As you noted, sometimes the file gets deleted anyway.  This could be the case here, and we're getting a 404 because the file did get deleted, but we're getting a retry bounce-back.

## Proposed resolutions

**On the simple side, add a `--delay`.**  Make it wait in between each delete.  This is too passive, though, to my thinking.

**Add retry and verification logic.** This is more involved. I can add some code to double-check that a snapshot is present, and attempt to perform the action again. This might warrant some "test for snapshot, attempt delete, and retry if fail," kind of logic.

Another thing occurred to me about validity of my approach. I need to exclude some endpoints from the authorization checks. I want implicit inclusion and explicit exclusion in authorization to avaid security issues by omission of including a handler in authorization.

So I don't only need to have a startup code for the middleware - I need it to be able to go through the handlers and decide whether it will wrap them or not. And my case is a bit more complex than looking up a handler function in a set to see if it's excluded:

```python
async def token_reading_middleware(app, handler):
    @wraps(handler)
    async def middleware_handler(request):
        # ...omitted...

    # Getting to the original functions, so we can disregard the wrappers that AioHTTP creates
    # and other potential middlewares on the processing chain.
    handler_original_function = _get_original_function(handler)
    non_secured_original_handlers = {_get_original_function(f)
                                     for f in app.get(NON_SECURED_HANDLERS_KEY, [])}
    if handler_original_function in non_secured_original_handlers:
        return handler
    return middleware_handler

def _get_original_function(function):
    """Assuming that `function` is wrapped in any level of decorators, this will return the original
    function without any decorators.
    """
    while hasattr(function, '__wrapped__'):
        function = function.__wrapped__
    return function
```

Another update on this on my side of things.  As @Houwang pointed out the browser does initially render the mathematics correctly but then appears to refresh and replace the initially rendered text with the `[Math Processing Error]` text.  I think this is just MathJax replacing the temporary images with the proper fonts.  I noticed in the error console though that the following files were not being found
```
[Error] Failed to load resource: the server responded with a status of 404 (NOT FOUND) (fontdata.js, line 0)
[Error] Failed to load resource: the server responded with a status of 404 (NOT FOUND) (BasicLatin.js, line 0)
[Error] Failed to load resource: the server responded with a status of 404 (NOT FOUND) (GeneralPunctuation.js, line 0)
```
In my case the "prime" `'` character was the culprit.  I first tried installing manually the latest version of MathJax via 
```
python -m IPython.external.mathjax ~/Downloads/MathJax-2.5-latest.zip` 
```
but that did not work as the version of MathJax being used, as nbgrader helpfully mentioned, was a static version in my anaconda installation.  In my case this was at 
```
~/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/notebook/static/components/MathJax/MathJax.js
```
Since I was not sure how to point nbgrader somewhere else I instead replaced the installation at the above path with a new (full?) version and everything works now!

I am not sure what the right way to correct this in general is at this point, is it possible to depend on the local IPython installation for MathJax instead of this static version or maybe fall back to one available on the web?

Here's an example of what this fix (combined with https://github.com/VirusTotal/yara/pull/723) addresses...

Given this python script:

```
import yara
from pprint import pprint

def modules_callback(data):
    a = 1/0
    print('hi')
    return yara.CALLBACK_CONTINUE

rule = 'import "dotnet" rule a { condition: false }'
rules = yara.compile(source=rule)
rules.match('/Users/wxs/malware/foo.exe', modules_callback=modules_callback)
```

If I run this script I would expect to get an exception thrown because of the divide by zero. Instead the exception is silently ignored. Note that to get this script to actually run you need to make sure you run ```rules.match``` on a .NET binary. If you only have a plain PE laying around just import that module and feed that PE file into ```rules.match```.

Thanks for catching that! By design, `autograd.grad` and `autograd.value_and_grad` only apply to functions with real-valued scalar outputs. I just added a check to `value_and_grad` so your example now throws an error.

If you're interested in complex derivatives of holomorphic functions, you can use `autograd.holomorphic_grad`. We don't have a `value_and_holomorphic_grad` (it would get out of hand if we created a 'value_and_*' for all the functions in autograd.differential_operators) but you can easily create your own using `grad`, `value_and_grad` and `holomorphic_grad` in [differential_operators.py](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/autograd/differential_operators.py) as examples. They're all simple wrappers around `autograd.core.make_vjp`. Hope that helps!

Cross posting this from https://github.com/OpenKMIP/PyKMIP/pull/60, since it's more appropriate here: 

Given the API interface is now called `KmipClient`, here are some alternative names for the actual `KmipClient`:

- `KmipClient` - we can leave it as is, since we can differentiate the interface and the class via imports
- `ProxyKmipClient` - designates that this client leverages the original proxy client underneath
- `PieKmipClient` - the first client representing the Pie API, seems odd to reference the package name in the client name but eh, whatever
- `BasicKmipClient` - designates that this client provides a simpler interface than the prior proxy client
- `SimpleKmipClient` - same as `BasicKmipClient` really, we considered this before but @rellerreller wasn't a fan
- `MinimalKmipClient` - designates that this client only provides basic CRUD operations for KMIP managed objects

I think users would be scared to use a `HazardousKmipClient`, just my $0.02.

Thoughts? Preferences? It's just a name find/replace to me. I kind of like `ProxyKmipClient` but `BasicKmipClient` and `MinimalKmipClient` would also work fine. Also tagging @joel-coffman in case he wants to chime in.

1) Encode the info in the scheme: `s3ssec://bucket/dir/file.ext` or `s5ec:…` (JK)
2) Encode the info in the query: `s3://bucket/dir/file.ext?encryption=SSE-C
3) Encode the info in the fragment: `s3://bucket/dir/file.ext#encryption=SSE-C

Don't like 3) because fragment is typically use to drill down within the resource, not select its format. Must research 2) if there is precedent or existing conventions. Well, in case of the following script:
```python
import json

from selenium import webdriver
from selenium.webdriver import DesiredCapabilities

caps = DesiredCapabilities.CHROME
caps["loggingPrefs"] = {"driver": "INFO"}
driver = webdriver.Chrome(desired_capabilities=caps,
                          service_log_path="service_log.txt")

driver.get("https://www.google.com")
with open("driver_log.txt", "w") as f:
  text = json.dumps(driver.get_log("driver"), indent=4)
  f.write(text)
driver.quit()
```

"service_log.txt" and "driver_log.txt" contain almost the same data. The only difference is that "service_log.txt" contains also `COMMAND InitSession`, `COMMAND Quit` and `RESPONSE Quit` that "driver_log.txt" does not contain.

But in case of Pytest-Selenium service_log does not contain the large portion of the end of the log.

I believe the reason is that Chromedriver buffers output that gets into service log.
I think Pytest-Selenium should get driver log only once (using `service_log_path` and after calling `driver.quit()`)

It looks like using a web service is generated and used different in .Net Core than it is in regular .Net and Core converts everything to async. So manipulation of the generated code has to happen which is ok.

First if you look at the CPU STacks you see that all the activity is in the first 6 seconds.    I am assuming that is expected.   It also looks like you are not CPU bound (most CPU time is spend in the idle process, you have an 8 proc system and are only using at most half of your available CPU).   The 'bro' and 'java' processes are fairly active, but the dotnet process consumes CPU in the start (500msec) and then uses very little.   This implies that you are not CPU bound but something else is slowing down the requests (thus this is probably not going to be a CPU investigation, which is unfortunate, as we don't really have good wall clock time investigation tools on Linux yet).  

Homing in on the dotnet process,  we see that libcoreclr.so!unknown is where most of the time is spent.  Thus we have a symbol problem (we need the symbol packages).  We have a plan to improve this experience, but for now you need to get symbols put in the right place.  Brian can help here.  however I don't think that this will help much as my expectation is that al tha time is JIT compile time.  Still having the symbols can help.  

However as mentioned, this is nto likely to be a CPU problem but some sort of blocked time problem.  To investigate this is is best to put in an System.Diagnostics.Tracing.EventSource and 'interesting' places so that we can see where time is spent (basically you get logging with accurate time stamps that let you break down the time).   

I would recommend using PerfView to collect a trace on Windows (simply do PerfView /threadTime collect).   Our instrumentation is MUCH better there and having a baseline of how it is SUPPOSED to work is very valuable.   

I would start there, as also instrument your code with an EventSource (see https://blogs.msdn.microsoft.com/vancem/2015/09/14/exploring-eventsource-activity-correlation-and-causation-features/), and get some measurements on Windows.   Then we can tell you how to turn this on for Linux and see what can be learned from that.  

However, it will take some effort.  You are on the bleading edge.  We want to make investigations like this much better, but Linux performance investigation is in its infancy.

Some of this is being changed for upcoming versions of .Net Core, see https://github.com/dotnet/designs/pull/29 and specifically [this document](https://github.com/dotnet/designs/blob/9a84136/proposals/sdk-version-scheme.md).

This is a known limitation that should perhaps be addressed in the documentation. Setting values in the configuration file is like setting them on the command line. On the command line, you have to specify the `domain` flag for each domain. For example:

`letsencrypt-auto --domain example1.com --domain example2.org`

This is what you would have to do in the configuration file BUT this doesn't quite work. If you do something like:
```
domains = example1.com
domains = example2.org
```
The value of `domains` is overwritten by "example2.org". I agree the use of `domains` is misleading and this limitation isn't ideal. Perhaps we could consider using a comma separated list for `--domains` in the future which would fix the problem.

Nice short question. Nope :-) I optimized it for medium size fast requests divided into chunks by 200 objects. I prefer SOAP API if `beatbox` is installed. The speed is around 200 objects per second and it is probably faster than BULK, up to about thousand objects. Bulk would be generally too slow for a web server or it would be unpredictable due to low priority. If about 10000 objects are inserted by SOAP I feel some slow down because I guess SFDC dislikes high average load on normal priority requests. It is still a good compromise for me also for data migration (in python shell or jupyter notebook). SOAP consumes one API call per 200 objects in governor limits statistics.

If beatbox is not installed, `composite batch` REST requests are used by 20 objects. I implemented it when batch was a pilot project without enough documentation. It is four times faster than individual insert, but was is a bad idea because it only encapsulates any independent requests and it uses one bid normal request splitted 20 to internal fast requests on SFDC, accounted as 21 API calls. It could be possible to use currently a Composite SObject Tree REST API request by 200 objects, probably similarly efficient to SOAP, but I'm not interested more after implementing SOAP.

If one row is inserted (by normal "save()" or by "bulk_create([one_ object])") a normal REST API is called.

(Django bulk_create converts the data to a list of ORM objects internally. Memory requirements could be a bottleneck for extra huge cases that really require bulk api.)

`env | grep …` is not a valid way to check locale when it comes to tmux: you need `tmux show-environment -g`. In a shell `locale` command is more convenient. Though based on what I see problem is that you have somehow run powerline-daemon with non-unicode locale, but current tmux server was first launched with unicode locale (meaning that powerline-config which is responsible for left arrows here faced unicode locale).

Running `powerline-daemon --replace` in a shell with unicode locale should fix the issue. Note that when tmux server dies powerline daemon does not, so if you happened to first run tmux in non-unicode locale restarting the server will not help. (Assuming you did not run powerline-daemon in non-unicode locale manually the only variant how you can get this result unintentionally I see is “running tmux with non-unicode locale, seeing absense of arrows, restarting tmux with unicode locale”.)

Below example is adding "extra" field after schema instance is initialized.
(If you want to define subclass like this, on __init__(), it is also ok.)

``` python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields


class S(Schema):
    v = fields.Integer(required=True)


print(S().load({"v": 10}))
print(S().load({"v": 10, "extra": "extra"}))

s = S()
s.fields["extra"] = fields.String(required=True)
print(s.load({"v": 10}))
print(s.load({"v": 10, "extra": "extra"}))

I think I will stay out of most of the discussion about structure and naming conventions, since I do not know what the standards and typical use-cases within the field are. 

However, it would be nice if the conventions are easy to automate in scripts. I'm pretty sure we were all already thinking along these lines, but I want to point out what this means for me.

The loom-viewer currently detects attributes that fit the conventions used in Sten's group, and adapts the interface to that for convenience:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/259840/32902739-7cb06278-caf3-11e7-9a7c-4c4e54e0d616.png)

Here it detected various attributes associated with X/Y position (in this case, previous "deprecated" conventions from before they "stabilised").

So good naming conventions/schemes will result in the loom viewer being more convenient to use too!

Regarding naming schemes: how about treating `_` as special "separator" symbol that signifies a relationship? This would only apply to the "keywords" of the naming conventions, of course, since people might also just use it as a surrogate for space to ensure compatibility. (I may be just repeating what Sten suggested about naming schemes in a different formulation)

A question is whether this relationshipship would be directed:  `{a}_{b}` could mean "a, subgroup b". It could also allow for nested grouping, which could be useful for building hierarchies: `Cluster_Layer1_ByLouvain` (I assume one want to group metadata by layers).

(I'll stop here before I propose something that ends up making the naming schemes Turing complete)

**Orientation:** note that this issue extends beyond loading data from loom files themselves: if each other package uses columns for genes in Python, that would require a transpose _each time_ we move data from one into the other. That is both slower and more error-prone. I do not know what the actual conventions in other packages are, but if I understand @falexwolf correctly the norm in software is column-oriented?

``total_irrad`` accepts scalar (fixed) or vector (tracking) surface azimuth and tilt. It does not automatically compute tracking angles, though.

My post above was a bit misleading. I suggested that ``globalinplane`` returns only POA global, but it actually returns POA global, POA direct, and POA diffuse (total). We could probably come up with a better name for ``globalinplane``. Something like ``poa_components``. 

Thinking through this a little more... ``total_irrad`` does 3 things:

First, ``total_irrad`` provides a unified interface to the various diffuse sky models. This aspect could be refactored into a separate function (``get_diffuse``?) and used within ``total_irrad``.

Second, ``total_irrad`` calls the ``grounddiffuse`` function.

Third, ``total_irrad`` assembles the POA components into a data structure (DataFrame or OrderedDict). ``total_irrad`` could be refactored to use a renamed ``globalinplane`` internally. The renamed ``globalinplane`` would need to include the sky diffuse and ground diffuse to its output for API consistency.

So, ``total_irrad`` would be reduced to a wrapper for the 3 steps described above. It would not do any calculations itself.

While we're at it, I'd welcome suggestions for a better name than ``total_irrad``.

For example, for declaring parameters, I use a custom generator : ```python import yaml from rest_framework.decorators import api_view, renderer_classes from rest_framework import response, schemas from rest_framework_swagger.renderers import OpenAPIRenderer, SwaggerUIRenderer from django.conf.urls import url, include from prototype.urls import router from rest_framework.compat import coreapi, urlparse class SchemaGenerator(schemas.SchemaGenerator): def get_link(self, path, method, view): """Custom the coreapi using the func.__doc__ . if __doc__ of the function exist, use the __doc__ building the coreapi. else use the default serializer. __doc__ in yaml format, eg: nota for type: Required. The type of the parameter. Since the parameter is not located at the request body, it is limited to simple types (that is, not an object). The value MUST be one of "string", "number", "integer", "boolean", "array" or "file". If type is "file", the consumes MUST be either "multipart/form-data", " application/x-www-form-urlencoded" or both and the parameter MUST be in "formData". --- desc: the desc of this api. ret: when success invoked, return xxxx err: when error occured, return xxxx input: - name: mobile desc: the mobile number type: string required: true location: form - name: promotion desc: the activity id type: integer required: true location: form """ fields = self.get_path_fields(path, method, view) func = getattr(view, view.action) if getattr(view, 'action', None) else None _method_desc = '' if func and func.__doc__: a = func.__doc__.split('---') if len(a) == 2: try: yaml_doc = yaml.load(a[1]) except: pass else: if 'desc' in yaml_doc: desc = yaml_doc.get('desc', '') ret = yaml_doc.get('ret', '') err = yaml_doc.get('err', '') _method_desc = desc + '\n
' + 'return: ' + ret + '
' + 'error: ' + err params = yaml_doc.get('input', []) for i in params: _name = i.get('name') _desc = i.get('desc') _required = i.get('required', True) _type = i.get('type', 'string') _location = i.get('location', 'form') field = coreapi.Field( name=_name, location=_location, required=_required, description=_desc, type=_type ) fields.append(field) else: _method_desc = a[0] fields += self.get_serializer_fields(path, method, view) else: fields += self.get_serializer_fields(path, method, view) fields += self.get_pagination_fields(path, method, view) fields += self.get_filter_fields(path, method, view) if fields and any([field.location in ('form', 'body') for field in fields]): encoding = self.get_encoding(path, method, view) else: encoding = None if self.url and path.startswith('/'): path = path[1:] return coreapi.Link( url=urlparse.urljoin(self.url, path), action=method.lower(), encoding=encoding, fields=fields, description=_method_desc ) @api_view() @renderer_classes([OpenAPIRenderer, SwaggerUIRenderer]) def schema_view(request): generator = SchemaGenerator(title='Product Testing API', patterns=[url(r'^v1/dreem/product_testing/', include([ url(r'^', include(router.urls)) ])),]) return response.Response(generator.get_schema(request=request)) ``` I allows me to write description as: ``` ''' desc ''' ``` or ``` ''' desc --- yaml desc ''' ``` In this last case, the desc is for developer and the yaml desc is for the documentation.

SMT2 format is much more general than DIMACS, so not all formulas can correspond to an equi-satisfiable DIMACS representation of a SAT problem. So assuming that SMT2 maps to DIMACS is in general false. For example, SMT2 allows you to write formulas over linear (and non-linear) arithmetic. There is no such direct representation in DIMACS for propositional logic. At best you can invoke some theorems that map ILP to a polynomial size representation in propositional logic and try your luck. Of course SMT solvers want to be less naïve and apply native procedures for theories, such as arithmetic.

For QF_FD (finite domain problems, that include bit-vectors and finite data-types) formulas can be converted to DIMACS. A future version of Z3 will have an option from the binary API to print goals to DIMACS. For formulas that go beyond QF_FD, the formulas will be abstractions. This can easily lead to confusion among use scenarios so I even wonder about the usefulness of this feature.

Note that you can use the binary API already to walk formulas that have been clausified and simplified, and bit-blasted and write your own pretty printer to DIMACS.

Well, first of all you need to compile a function for computing predictions:
```
pred_fn = theano.function([input_var], test_prediction)
```
This assumes everything is defined as in the example (i.e., you have built the network, loaded the weights from the model file, and defined the `test_prediction` expression).

To load the image, the first result in a major search engine is: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31386096/importing-png-files-into-numpy
Note that it will have to match the size of the MNIST digits used for training, i.e., 24x24 grayscale, otherwise you need to convert it (e.g., with PIL, the Python Imaging Library).

Finally, compute the prediction as `pred_fn(your_image[np.newaxis, np.newaxis])` -- the `np.newaxis` will insert two dimensions in front of the height and width of your image, so it has a batch size of 1 and channel count of 1.

True, we will still need to wrap that separately. I would argue in favour of keeping this as a separate class, because of the different memory layout (which we handle with an optional `dimshuffle` flag) and the various limitations associated with it.

> Did you time cuda convnet vs gpucorrmm on GTX580? I think it is the only GPU where
cuda convnet could be usefull. On kepler and fermi GPU, I'm pretty sure
cudnn is faster then cuda convnet.

I don't have a GTX 580 on hand. But I know for a fact that cuda-convnet (v1, the one wrapped in pylearn2) is faster than CuDNN v2 on the GTX 980 for some configurations (like 20-40% faster, so it's definitely significant). I think this is because neither are optimized for Maxwell, and the cuda-convnet code just happens to translate better. (It's possible that cuda-convnet v2 is even faster, but that isn't available in Theano right now.)

This will most likely change when CuDNN v3 is released, since Maxwell optimizations are something they are supposedly working on.

The proof of concept you have created here is very helpful and I really appreciate all the effort you have put into putting this together! 

The problem you are trying to solve is quite challenging because there are so many different moving parts involved, from how we handle data in the API to how we write data and how HDF5 handles the data. Since the problem of multiple data streams is fundamental to a number of use cases, I really want us to get this right (or as close to right as possible). Let me suggest the following course of action:

- It would be helpful if you could outline the use-case you are trying to solve. You mentioned #309 that you want to integrate your data pipeline. How does this pipeline look? How many data streams are involved? How large are the data streams? Do you want to write the data to one or multiple files (and why)? etc. Having this information would help us better understand the requirements when we try to define a design to solve the problem. 
- I will create a couple of issues to try to break down the problem into more manageable chunks. I will reference pull request #310 and #309 issues there to make sure we don't lose track of our discussions. 
- @ajtritt and I will get together to try to come up with a design that will address these issues as well as how to use it to address the larger issue. 
- Once I have created the issues I would close this pull request. We'll keep you in the loop as we go through the process to make this work.

No tutorial to do specifically that exists... What you would want to do, from the angr level, is:

1. Create an otiegnqwvk (http://angr.io/api-doc/angr.html#angr.exploration_techniques.ExplorationTechnique) that represents your analysis method. Naive symbolic execution on coreutils will get overwhelmed almost immediately (while we're at it, so will veritesting). You will definitely need to design some sort of prioritization and path filtering system. This can be as trivial as just keeping a random N of all the paths, but it should be something.
2. Run a PathGroup with that exploration technique to test naive symbolic execution.
3. Run a PathGroup with that exploration technique *and* a nested veritesting technique to test veritesting.
4. Compare.

The core take-away is that you'll need something like an ExplorationTechnique to guide your analysis.

With coreutils, you will also run into a lot of gotchas related to missing environment models (syscalls). You can read about these gotchas here: http://docs.angr.io/docs/gotchas.html

> even small executable like `ls`, it takes forever

`ls` is not as small as the example (fauxware) as I was using. `CFGAccurate` is several orders of magnitude slower than `CFGFast` since it simulates the execution of basic blocks. Additionally, raising context sensitivity will lead to an (roughly) exponential increase in the number of basic blocks that you recover.

My suggestion is to limit `CFGAccurate` on a subgraph, like several functions, to make it more tractable.

Not sure what happened there, possibly the result of cherry-picking. The minor bug I spotted was that the put and WN script behaviour was completely inconsistent with the DIRAC upload at the end of the job. These should now be in sync.

1. The simple answer is to check for these things earlier than post-boostrap. Any consistency checks can be performed *during* bootstrap, while the plugin is being loaded and safely exit there.
2. The config files (including `~/.gangarc`) are loaded during `configure()`. Any entries which are not understood (due to them having not been loaded in a pugin yet) are cached until the appropriate `addOption()` call is made. So by line 85 in `GangaDirac/__init__.py` you can check config values and correctly see any config file overrides.

There is a possible question about `copyTo` implementation here:

1. Have `get` call `copyTo` with the `localDir` as the argument
2. Have `copyTo` call `get` if the file isn't already in `localDir` (~current impl)

I have a leaning towards 2) as if the file is already present you don't have to grab it again (with option 1 you would 'download' every time). However, this has the issue of telling `get` where to copy the file to. I'm actually in favour of **not** changing `localDir` before calling `get` here as this is abusing what `get` is supposed to do (plus it would need to be exception save to avoid problems during the `get` and being left with a changed `localDir`). I think it's better to just call `get` within `copyTo` to ensure the file is at `localDir` and then do a local file copy.

What are people's thoughts?

Summarizing from discussion over Slack. The fix works well for the case service worker used on: https://n0tan3rd.github.io/replay_test but actually identified two other issues!

1) The `init_server_worker_override()` in wombat.js is supposed to be setting the modifier to `id_` which should prevent any rewriting for being applied to service workers. However, since only a filename is used, the link is not being rewritten. If it had worked as expected, this fix actually would not have applied to the service worker on https://n0tan3rd.github.io/replay_test

2) Ideally, there would be a separate modifier just for service workers, so that standard document insertion JS would not needed to be applied. Perhaps `sw_`. This modifier could then be used to rewrite scripts that are used as service workers, and this change could be added only for those scripts. This would mean adding a new modifier and is more flexible in the long run.

Another hotfix for this is to exclude templates used by `StructBlock` from debug toolbar. E.g.

```
class MyStructBlock(StructBlock):
    ...
    class Meta:
        ...
        template = 'myblocks/structblock.html'
```

And add to `settings.py` file:
```
DEBUG_TOOLBAR_CONFIG = {
    'SKIP_TEMPLATE_PREFIXES': ('django/forms/widgets/', 'admin/widgets/', 'myblocks/structblock.html'),
}
```

From DDT doc for `SKIP_TEMPLATE_PREFIXES`:

> Templates starting with those strings are skipped when collecting rendered templates and contexts. Template-based form widgets are skipped by default because the panel HTML can easily grow to hundreds of megabytes with many form fields and many options.

We thought about this in the past and decided not to compute the noise floor based on sampling the RSSI.  Some of the challenges that came out:

1. There is little guidance on how to compute the noise floor based on samples.  For example, how often should samples be performed?  What is the duration of the averaging window?  If something like exponentially-weighted moving average is used, what are the coefficients?  The specific implementation necessarily needs to find a happy medium on being responsive, but not overly reactive.
2. Sampling the noise floor on a sleepy device is energy intensive and may not provide significant added value.

For this reason, we decided to take the initial path where the Link Margin is computed against the device's receive sensitivity, which is hardware-specific and provides a lower bound on the noise floor.  It is also the same reason why the Thread Specification chose the term "Link Margin" rather than "Signal-to-Noise Ratio".  Having said that, I agree we need to enhance the platform code to provide a receive sensitivity value.

Happy to entertain other proposals to enhance the link metric computation.

@ktbyers I might have confused everyone (probably because I myself was confused)

- **autoComplete: false**:

_Simple short hand fail:_
Example:
```
sh ver
**Error**
```

_A short handed command in any configuration mode will fail_
Example:
```
router bgp 1
router 192.168.1.1
**Error**
```
Here we have the command router-id in BGP config mode. So the EOS considers we are using this command. But since the autoComplete is off, it will throw error


_But if there is no such command in the current configuration mode, we try to match the previous configuration mode_

Example:
```
interface Ethernet 1
router bgp 1
**Valid**
```
Here router command is not valid under interface configuration. So the EOS tries the global configuration mode (Please note that it should match exactly in the global config mode)


- **autoComplete: true** 

_Simple short hand works in any mode_
Example:
```
router bgp 1
router 192.168.1.1
**Valid**
```

_If the command is not valid in current configuration mode we check for previous config mode_(like when autoComplete is false)
```
interface Ethernet 1
router bgp 1
**Valid**
```

_Now if the command is incomplete, the EOS tries to match the next parameter_
Example
```
router bgp 1
router 192.168.1.1
router multicast
**Valid**
```

Here the router 192.168.1.1 command matches router-id configuration. And the router multicast will match the global configuration (although this can be matched for router-id, the second argument is not a router id format)


- **expandAliases**

We can create aliases in EOS. for example let's say I create alias to check non-zero interface counters
```
alias shnz show interfaces counters | nz
```

Now on the EOS you can just say `shnz` to run the above command. To use the `shnz` as a command from pyeapi, we need to give the `expandAliases` option

Hope this explains it.

Anymail extends Django's [`EmailMessage`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/email/#the-emailmessage-class) class, which is specced to take a single string as its `from_email`. Pybind11 will run its own (improved) version of `find_package(PythonLibs)` internally (see [here](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/tools/FindPythonLibsNew.cmake)). If `PYTHONLIBS_FOUND` is already true, it will not overwrite an existing configuration. I guess the solution here is to just not use `find_package(PythonLibs)` before `add_subdirectory(pybind11)` or if you must, use pybind11's version:

```cmake
list(APPEND CMAKE_MODULE_PATH "pybind11/tools")  # make FindPythonLibsNew.cmake visible
find_package(PythonLibsNew 3.5 REQUIRED) 
```

A few comments (for now):

- It seems like it's going to be a fair amount of work, and going to have some backwards compatibility concerns.  Have you considered an alternative of using the `maybe<T>` as an implementation detail of the type caster, i.e. so that instead of declaring `T value;` you declare `maybe<T> value;`?  That avoids the default construction, but requires a less drastic change.  I'm not opposed to the more drastic change, but just throwing this out there.

- the backwards compatibility issue might be addressed with the move to `pybind11::caster` namespace: Keep a `type_caster<T, SFINAE>` in `detail`, but make the base case a special has-no-caster object.  Then the new base template in `caster` can, if a old-style `detail` caster exists, load it through some compatibility object that converts it via construct-and-load into a `maybe<T, appropriate_policy>`.

- One thing that would be nice for type casters is to be able to return more than just yes/no: to return no with a failure message.  So, for example, suppose I have two Eigen-accepting functions, `f(Eigen::Vector3d)`, `f(Eigen::Matrix3d)`, and I try to call `f()` with a 2x2 numpy array.  Right now, I get:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: f(): incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
    1. (arg0: numpy.ndarray[float64[3, 3]]) -> None
    2. (arg0: numpy.ndarray[float64[3, 1]]) -> None
    3. (arg0: numpy.ndarray[float64[m, n]], arg1: int) -> None

Invoked with: array([[ 1.,  2.],
       [ 3.,  4.]])
```

but it would be very nifty to be able to get something like:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: f(): incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
    1. (arg0: numpy.ndarray[float64[3, 3]]) -> None
       - arg0: incompatible matrix dimensions: expected 3x3, received 2x2
    2. (arg0: numpy.ndarray[float64[3, 1]]) -> None
       - arg0: incompatible matrix dimensions: expected 3x1, received 2x2

Invoked with: array([[ 1.,  2.],
       [ 3.,  4.]])
```
With `maybe<T>` this sort of thing could be done easily: it could have success, fail, and fail-with-error-string states.  Arguments that fail without a message could get a generic "- argn: incompatible argument" message.  (Another way I thought about doing this would be through throw a special exception object, but that would have the significant disadvantage of only being able to show one argument failure per call, even if multiple occurred).

I've made more progress on this. On the `bmw_multiple_vhosts` branch, I have a fix for finding vhosts contained in blocks such as `IfModule` and `Macro` in a file with multiple vhosts.

While we now properly find these vhosts, we often copy the wrong vhost in `_copy_create_vhost_skeleton` in such situations. This is because Augeas paths for these vhosts look like `/files/path/to/vhost/IfModule/VirtualHost` or `/files/path/to/vhost/IfModule[1]/VirtualHost` without the square brackets on the end of the path like our logic currently checks for.

I have a rough plan to fix that but it's bit tricky. The solution I have in mind looks something like:

1. Strip off `/files/path/to/vhost` from Augeas path.
2. Split the remaining path into directive name and index tuples. If there is no index, the index is 1 because Augeas indices start at 1. I plan on checking for the index using something like `re.match(".*\[\d+\]$", directive)`. If anyone has a reason I can get false positives using this regex on Augeas paths, please let me know.
3. Skip all parts of the file until we hit the first directive for our vhost. After we do this, we copy everything to the matching closing directive except we skip over any VirtualHosts other than the one we care about (as well as things like certain `RewriteRules` which we already skip). After the matching closing directive, we skip the rest of the file.

For simplicity and (I believe in some cases correctness), I'm planning on even doing this for files with a single vhost. This changes our behavior slightly as we will no longer copy directives or comments outside of the first `<Directive>` that directly affects the vhost we're copying.

I've started implementing this but I don't have anything close to ready. If anyone has any feedback on the plan, please let me know.

For a bit of relevant history, when Certbot launched, one of its biggest criticisms that's largely relevant here is that Certbot didn't follow the UNIX philosophy and was prioritizing the wrong features and adding bloat. Rather than a small lightweight tool that sysadmins can use in their systems and build off of (keeping in mind that sysadmins are largely the target audience for Certbot), we had this giant catch all tool trying to work for everybody and their grandma (to paraphrase a particular comment I remember). This criticism (as well as other issues) was so bad it caused core contributors to the project to split off and make their own project (https://github.com/kuba/simp_le).

Around that time, I had many conversations with people about a much more minimal and modular Certbot. Unfortunately, I don't see us getting the OK from management to spend time ourselves in investing in significant rewrites like this, but I really appreciate your thoughts here and this is the kind of thing we should be considering when Certbot is in a position to make breaking changes like this.

Are you talking about if you have something like iptables redirecting all ported traffic or if your webserver configuration specifies that port 80 traffic should go to 443?
In essence webroot will use your existing server setup to serve challenge files at `YOURURL.TLD/.well-known/CHALLENGEHASH` which will be read by let's encrypt.
In this case if your server is redirecting traffic, that file will still be able to be read and you will receive a certificate.
In the other case (where you don't have a webserver running or don't have one running on 443) then standalone will spin up a webserver and serve a challenge over 443.
If you already have nginx or apache installed, then certbot can install your certificate via the apache or nginx plugin.

Yesterday, successfuly did some transfers with @kelsos , and after that, closed the channel. After close transactions were mined (but before settle), shut down my node to go home. After restarting, it seems the some of the messages (even ones that seems were successfully handled in yesterday's session) were kept in the `queueids_to_queues` passed to transport's `start` method, which then sent them, but as the channel was closed, even if the partner were online, they may not respond to it, and without a `Delivered` message, our node would keep sending then each `50s` (10 * retry_timeout).
#2081 should fix it, and [matrix shouldn't also use these delivered messages to retry messages](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/commit/9e8facf8f9dacf64e75f00ca255ecc52c4b79916#r30086619), but we should also have some logic to clean expired/invalid messages from the queue (valid ones are already cleaned on `Delivered` state change), and transport should not continue sending them after they were cleaned.

I'll have to think about that... Right now I have a feeling requiring a dict might slightly simplify some use cases, which are not very likely to start with, while complicating general usage and the internal code and also might make future expansions more complicated.

If a dict representation of the options is actually required, we could always implement a "dict()" method for our options object, so users could just do
```Python
options = scour.options()
dict_representation = options.dict()
```
(This is one of the expansions which would not be possible if we require a dict).

Edit: Well, it's hard to know in what ways Scour might be used by external applications. For me the aim was to offer a implementation that makes a "python app using scour as python lib" as easy as possible and would allow to be adjusted to our needs in future without breaking backwards.compatibility.

CLI usage is a question I could not solve myself yet: On the one hand we offer CLI already, on the other hand third-party apps might want to implement their own. Do we want to expose parts of the CLI functionality already present in Scour? Or should we make that part completely internal?  
I guess without actual usage scenarios it will be hard to design these parts of the API. Then again I assume usage scenarios will develop around whatever API we create, so the most flexible approach might just be the one to choose...

The problem with file tracking is that many times when we run a command with many output, we only pick an representative one as the result of the step. So 

1. `sos pack` tends to miss some files
2. `sos pack --include` can be long and still miss something. 
3. If we manually remove unwanted files, `sos pack` lacks an `--all` option, and even with it there is no advantage of `sos pack` over `tar czf`.

The "packing environment" proposal is reasonable but I am not aware of any way to do it because of all the commands and their dependencies. The closest thing is singularity to packing commands and data together for execution, but that is for another use case.

@concretevitamin Thanks for looking into this! Here's what I got:

- I recompiled from master with the experimental flag and ran the script again, with the `flush_period_secs=0.1` argument. I got the same ~4.8GB figure at the 4 million step on the **single** redis-server process spawned by `ray`, same as you got.

- I then ran again the experiment with the binary release and no experimental features and I noticed that `ray` now spawns **two** redis processes one using ~4GB and the other 3.4GB at the 4 million step. These really are different `redis-server` procs, the ports are different, I'm not talking about the threads spawned by `redis-server`. Furthermore I recall seeing four such processes on the 128GB machine two weeks ago.

So, for me at least, the experimental features seem to force only one redis process which indeed uses less memory than the two combined. However it didn't appear to me that the memory usage stabilizes at a given point. I will confirm this on the larger machine (I was running out of memory on the 16GB one and I had to stop it at around 4 million steps.).

Furthermore I am close to finishing an implementation resembling Ape-X and I'll report on a real-life situation.

That's *extremely* strange. I've tried your example, but it works for me.

My best guess is the following. Maybe you are using two different commits of Ray on the two machines. If the commits of Ray are the same, it's possible that the commits of Arrow in `ray/src/numbuf/thirdparty/arrow` are slightly different. Can you check that?

I'd suggest doing `rm -rf ray/` and `pip uninstall ray` on both machines. Then re-clone the repository on both machines and reinstall. That might fix it.

Beyond that, I have a few questions:

1. Does the problem happen **consistently** for you on two nodes? That is, does it happen every single time you do the above experiment?
2. Does it **ever** happen if you use just one node? Or does the problem seem to **require** two nodes? Can you try **both** nodes individually and see if the problem can be made to appear?
3. Is there a chance you have slightly different versions of Python on the two machines (or two different versions of something else)? Not that that would explain this behavior.

Hi @mitar. Thanks for the suggestion. What you are referring to is an attribute-based hard constraint. Internally, our scheduling algorithms (both local and global) already support attribute-based constraints, but adding *new* types of attributes, at the moment, requires adding an entry to the enum type and recompiling. So label support is not user-ready in its generality. Once we add a configuration manager (like etcd), we'll look into adding more general support for labels/attributes at that time. If you'd like a specific use case supported in the meantime, we could walk through what that entails.

Just thought about the version string again. I'm not sure if this belongs into this PR, but I also didn't want to open a new issue...

The Streamlink version that's being returned by `--loglevel debug` and `--version` is always the version string of the latest release. It'll never show the commit id if Streamlink has been built from git or if it's a nightly build on Windows. I know, this has always been the case, but since we're trying to improve the issue reports here, I think it would be nice to use a better version string.

Btw, the version constant is still being exported (and [imported](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.9.0/src/streamlink_cli/argparser.py#L202)) as [`LIVESTREAMER_VERSION`](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.9.0/src/streamlink_cli/constants.py#L3) from the [`__version__`](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.9.0/src/streamlink/__init__.py#L25) constant that gets updated on every release.

```
<?php
$queryString = $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'];

$cmd = "/usr/bin/streamlink -O $url best";
header('Content-type: video/mpeg');
header("Content-Type: application/octet-stream");
passthru($cmd);
?>
```

into apache config/virtual-host (/etc/apache2/sites-enabled/000-default.conf on ubuntu)
```
Alias /streamlink /var/www/streamlink/index.php

when a stream switches from **not blocked** to **geo blocked**

ORF replaces the whole stream with a geo blocked video,
which won't play in streamlink because it's data number is different than the stream before so it's invalid for streamlink.

such as TV shows that they are not allowed to air on the internet.

Example script that shows how you might use this:

```python
from trio import run, open_nursery, Event, sleep
from trio.trace import pausing, trace


e = Event()


async def foo():
    await trace("start of foo")
    print("in foo")


@run
async def main():
    print("starting")
    async with open_nursery() as n:

        n.start_soon(foo)

        async with pausing('start of foo'):
            print("in pausing block")

            await sleep(1)

            print("finishing pausing block")

        print("end of pausing block")
        await sleep(1)
```

> This should be doable since resume_from(...) should be capable of loading multiple checkpoint files. 

Right, "loading multiple checkpoint files" is the functionality I was advocating for, I'm glad to hear it's already part of the plan. This is minor, but in my opinion `load_cache(red, blue)` or `load_checkpoints(A, B)` would more clearly describe what's being done-- `resume_from` for me implies something being done in serial, it's not clear what `resume_from(red, blue)` means (are we starting from red or blue?).

> The yellow workflow has to be a mashup of parts of both the blue and red workflow to use results from either since the results are made available only when the parent apps are called.

Yes, exactly. In my work, it's a common scenario. Checkpointing "red workflow" so that "red workflow" can be restarted without waste is exciting. Checkpointing "red workflow" so work done can be re-used in subsequent workflows is still more exciting! In HEP this is commonly achieved "by hand" by splitting work into stages depending on how often you're likely to make changes to each stage. That way the less-frequently-changing stages don't have to be re-run. Parsl supporting multiple checkpoint files would abstract that "by hand" factorization away.

> checkpointing is closely related to provenance tracking and monitoring, and it might help to design the interface keeping future deliverables like pushing state info to a DB

I was assuming that the checkpoint object would stored in a DB. For optimal speed I think that's the way to go. What is the current proposal?

> do we include a previous checkpoint in a run that loads from a checkpoint ?

This is an important question and a bit tricky. The more unneeded "stuff" that's being carried around in the checkpoints, the slower/less convenient they will be. I think we would definitely want to at least have the option to "prune" a checkpoint such that anything from the previous checkpoint that is not used in the current workflow is dropped.

Expanding on Emad's response above:

Ideally, it would be nice if RNN, LSTM, GRU can all be expressed as instances of the same loop/recurrence with a different body/cell: say, as Recurrence<RNN-Cell>, Recurrence<LSTM-Cell>, and Recurrence<GRU-Cell>(where Recurrence could be a Loop or a more specialized operator).

Each of RNN-Cell, LSTM-Cell, and GRU-Cell can, in turn, be defined in terms of more primitive operations (as in their corresponding equations), which can be represented as as graphs, say RNN-Cell-Graph, LSTM-Cell-Graph, and GRU-Cell-Graph.

Without named functions, we will end up duplicating RNN-Cell-Graph for every RNN (loss of a concise representation in the model), or ending up adding an operator called RNN (expanding the set of operators).

So, the proposal allows us to:
(a) Accommodate newer recurrent layers (for example),
(b) Without requiring all implementations to directly implement the new layer,
(c) While keeping models compact

There are python protobuf package and system protobuf package. They don't need to be of the same version. However, the system protobuf package it provides protoc, protobuf headers and protobuf libraries files (e.g. libprotobuf.so), these files need to be from the same version in a build.

I've been playing around with this some and added a test here: https://github.com/fastavro/fastavro/pull/218/

As you can see from the [travis output](https://travis-ci.org/fastavro/fastavro/builds/392863364), this works on python 3.7 but fails on all other versions. This is because the ability to call `seek` and `tell` were just recently added: https://bugs.python.org/issue22908.

I don't want to revert our usage of `tell` that was added recently, but as a workaround you should be able to do the following:

```python
import fastavro
from zipfile import ZipFile
from io import BytesIO


with ZipFile('weather.avro.zip', 'r') as fo:
    with fo.open('weather.avro', 'r') as zo:
        reader = fastavro.reader(BytesIO(zo.read()))  # <--- wrap zo
        for data in reader:
            print(data)
```
